```mermaid
    G -- No --> I[Step I: Cascade Absorbing State Reached]
    I --> J[Step J: Measure Damage - S, E, R]
    J --> K[Step K: Compare with alpha_c threshold]
```

## 4.2 Motter–Lai Load Redistribution Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Node A: Source Load LA] --> X[Block X: Load Redistribution Mechanism]
    nodeB[Node B: Source Load LB] --> X
    nodeC[Node C: Source Load LC] --> X
    X --> checkA[Check A: LA new vs CA capacity]
    X --> checkB[Check B: LB new vs CB capacity]
    X --> checkC[Check C: LC new vs CC capacity]
    checkA -->|Fail| failA[Node A: REMOVED]
    checkA -->|Survive| liveA[Node A: Survives]
    checkB -->|Fail| failB[Node B: REMOVED]
    checkB -->|Survive| liveB[Node B: Survives]
    checkC -->|Fail| failC[Node C: REMOVED]
    checkC -->|Survive| liveC[Node C: Survives]
    failA -.-> X
    failB -.-> X
    failC -.-> X
```

## 4.3 Interdependent Network Cascade (Cyber-Physical System)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Power Grid Network] <-->|Bidirectional Dependency| C[Communication Network]
    P --> P1[Node P1: Generator]
    P --> P2[Node P2: Transformer]
    P --> P3[Node P3: Substation]
    C --> C1[Node C1: Router]
    C --> C2[Node C2: Server]
    C --> C3[Node C3: Switch]
    P1 -.depends on.-> C2
    P2 -.depends on.-> C3
    C1 -.depends on.-> P2
    C2 -.depends on.-> P3
    P3 -.depends on.-> C1
    C3 -.depends on.-> P1
```

## 4.4 Phase Transition Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SingleNet[Single Network Percolation]
        direction LR
        A1[Order Parameter S] --> B1[Continuous Rise]
        A1 --> C1[Second Order Transition]
        C1 --> D1[Critical Point p_c equals 1 over k]
    end
    subgraph InterNet[Interdependent Network]
        direction LR
        A2[Order Parameter S] --> B2[Abrupt Drop to Zero]
        A2 --> C2[First Order Transition]
        C2 --> D2[Critical Point p_c approx 2.4 over k]
    end
```

## 4.5 Block Architecture of a Cascade Simulation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[Block 1: Network Topology Input N, E] --> Loader[Block 2: Load Calculator L_i equals Betweenness]
    Loader --> Capacity[Block 3: Capacity Assigner C_i equals 1+alpha times L_i]
    Capacity --> Trigger[Block 4: Failure Trigger Mechanism]
    Trigger --> Remover[Block 5: Node Removal Engine]
    Remover --> Redistr[Block 6: Load Redistribution Engine]
    Redistr --> Monitor[Block 7: State Monitor - Track S, E, R]
    Monitor --> Decision[Block 8: Cascade Termination Check]
    Decision -->|Continue| Remover
    Decision -->|Stop| Output[Block 9: Output - Final Network State]
    Output --> Metrics[Block 10: Robustness Metrics R, alpha_c]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]
**Define cascading failure in complex networks. List the three essential ingredients of any cascade model.**

**Model Answer:**

A **cascading failure** is a sequential propagation of component failures in a network, in which the failure of one node (or edge) triggers the failure of others through redistribution of load, flow, or dependencies.

The three essential ingredients of any cascade model are:
1. **Load definition** — how stress is quantified (e.g., betweenness centrality).
2. **Capacity constraint** — maximum load each node can tolerate.
3. **Redistribution rule** — how load from a failed node is reassigned to neighbors.

(3 marks: 1 for definition + 1 each for the three ingredients, but allocate as 1+1+1)

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024]
**State and explain the difference between first-order and second-order phase transitions in cascading failure models on interdependent and single networks.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Single Networks:** Under random node removal, the size of the largest connected component $S$ decreases **continuously** to zero at the percolation threshold $p_c = 1/\langle k \rangle$. This is a **second-order (continuous) phase transition**.

- **Interdependent Networks:** When two networks mutually depend on each other, a small failure can trigger a **catastrophic, discontinuous collapse** of the giant component. The transition is **first-order**, with $p_c \approx 2.4/\langle k \rangle$, much larger than the single-network case.

The key difference: a small perturbation in an interdependent system can cause **complete fragmentation** — a phenomenon impossible in single-layer networks.

(3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute betweenness** after node removal (cost: −2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up first-order and second-order** phase transitions (cost: −2 marks).
> 3. **Not stating units** of load/capacity in derivations (cost: −1 mark).
> 4. **Confusing $L_i$ and $C_i$** in failure criterion $L_i > C_i$ (cost: −2 marks).
> 5. **Skipping the termination condition** in cascade loop (cost: −1 mark).

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Motter–Lai model** for cascading failures. Clearly define the load, capacity, and failure criterion. Show the relationship between the tolerance parameter $\alpha$ and network robustness.

**(b)** [7 Marks] For a path graph $P_5$ with nodes $\{1,2,3,4,5\}$ and tolerance $\alpha = 0.4$, calculate the initial loads (betweenness) of all nodes. Then simulate the cascade triggered by removal of node 3. Show all intermediate steps and determine the surviving network.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Modified]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **CASCADE model** for power transmission grids. Derive the recursion relation for branch load and discuss the conditions under which a cascade grows versus dies out.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Consider a star graph $K_{1,4}$ with central node $c$ and leaves $\{l_1, l_2, l_3, l_4\}$. The tolerance parameter is $\alpha = 0.5$. (i) Compute initial loads. (ii) Determine the critical $\alpha$ at which the cascade triggered by removing the central node stops fragmenting the network.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A:

**(a) Motter–Lai Model Description:**

The Motter–Lai model quantifies cascading failures via **betweenness centrality**.

**Load Definition:**
$$L_i = B_i = \sum_{s \neq t} \frac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$$

**Capacity Definition:**
$$C_i = (1 + \alpha) \cdot L_i(0), \quad \alpha \geq 0$$

**Failure Criterion:** Node $i$ fails at time $t$ if $L_i(t) > C_i$.

**Cascade Loop:**
1. Compute initial $L_i(0) = B_i$ for all nodes on $G$.
2. Compute $C_i = (1+\alpha) L_i(0)$ for all nodes.
3. Remove an initial node $i_0$ (attack or random failure).
4. Recompute betweenness on damaged network $G'$.
5. Check for overloaded nodes. Remove them.
6. Repeat from step 4 until no more failures.
7. Measure surviving giant component $S$.

**Robustness vs. $\alpha$:**
- For $\alpha < \alpha_c$ (critical tolerance), the network fragments catastrophically.
- For $\alpha \geq \alpha_c$, the network remains connected.
- $\alpha_c$ depends on the network topology:
$$\alpha_c = \max_{i} \frac{B_i^{(1)} - B_i}{B_i}$$

(7 marks: 1 for diagram description + 2 for load definition + 2 for capacity + 2 for cascade loop)

**(b) Numerical Simulation on $P_5$:**

**Step 1: Initial Betweenness**

For path graph $P_5$, betweenness is computed by counting pairs (s,t) whose shortest path passes through $i$:
- $B_1 = 0$
- $B_2 = 3$ (pairs: (1,3), (1,4), (1,5))
- $B_3 = 6$ (pairs: (1,4), (1,5), (2,4), (2,5), (4,1), (5,1) — count is 4, wait recalculate)

**Recalculation for $P_5$ (nodes 1-2-3-4-5):**
All ordered pairs (s,t) with $s \neq t$ = $5 \times 4 = 20$ ordered pairs. For an undirected graph in betweenness, we count unordered pairs: $\binom{5}{2} = 10$ pairs.

For each pair, the shortest path is unique. Through node $i$, count pairs $(s,t)$ where the unique path passes through $i$:

- $B_1 = 0$ (endpoint)
- $B_2$: paths passing through 2 → pairs with one endpoint $\leq 1$ and other $\geq 3$ → (1,3), (1,4), (1,5) → 3 pairs.
- $B_3$: pairs (1,4), (1,5), (2,4), (2,5) → 4 pairs.
- $B_4$: pairs (1,3), (1,2) wait, (1,3) is via 2, not 4. Pairs through 4 → (1,5), (2,5), (1,4)? No, (1,4) goes 1-2-3-4, through 4 as endpoint. Through 4 means strictly between → pairs (s,t) with $s \leq 3$ and $t = 5$: (1,5), (2,5), (3,5) → 3 pairs.
- $B_5 = 0$ (endpoint)

So: $B = [0, 3, 4, 3, 0]$.

**Step 2: Initial Capacity (with $\alpha = 0.4$):**
- $C_1 = 0$, $C_2 = 4.2$, $C_3 = 5.6$, $C_4 = 4.2$, $C_5 = 0$.

**Step 3: Remove Node 3.**

Network becomes two components: $\{1,2\}$ and $\{4,5\}$.

**Step 4: Recompute Betweenness on Damaged Network:**
- For $\{1,2\}$: only pair (1,2), path is 1-2. $B_1' = 0$, $B_2' = 0$ in the new graph. (No pair has 2 strictly between.)

Actually, $B_i$ in the damaged graph: node 2 is endpoint of $\{1,2\}$ subgraph → $B_2' = 0$. Node 4 is endpoint of $\{4,5\}$ subgraph → $B_4' = 0$.

**Step 5: Check Failure Condition:**
- $B_2' = 0 \leq 4.2$ ✓ (survives)
- $B_4' = 0 \leq 4.2$ ✓ (survives)

**Step 6: Cascade Terminates.**

**Final Surviving Network:** Two disconnected components $\{1,2\}$ and $\{4,5\}$.

**Conclusion:** The cascade from removing node 3 disconnects the path graph but does not destroy all nodes. The network is **fragmented but not completely destroyed**. (7 marks: 2 for betweenness + 1 for capacity + 2 for cascade simulation + 2 for conclusion)

---

### Solution to Question B:

**(a) CASCADE Model Explanation:**

The **CASCADE** (Curtailing Authority to Set Aside Critical Events) model by Dobson, Carreras, and Newman treats each transmission branch as a quasi-static load-bearing element.

**Model Equations:**

The initial load on branch $n$ is $L_n^{(0)}$ (a random variable, often uniform on $[L^{\min}, L^{\max}]$).

After an initiating disturbance adds $\Delta L$ to all branches, the new load is:
$$L_n^{(1)} = L_n^{(0)} + \Delta L$$

If $L_n^{(1)} > L_n^{\text{fail}}$ (failure threshold), branch $n$ fails and its load $\Delta P$ is distributed to **all other branches**. After $k$ failures in total:
$$L_n = L_n^{(0)} + \Delta L + k \cdot \Delta P$$

**Recursion Relation:**
$$L_{n+1} = L_n + \Delta P$$
until the cascade stops when no more branches exceed their threshold.

**Conditions for Cascade Growth vs. Decay:**

- If the average load increment $\Delta P$ is small and $\Delta L$ is small, the cascade dies quickly (**subcritical** regime).
- If $\Delta P \cdot (\text{number of failed branches})$ exceeds the **slack** ($L_n^{\text{fail}} - L_n$) of surviving branches, the cascade **grows** (**supercritical**).
- The transition is governed by the **expected propagation rate** $\mu = \Delta P / \langle L_n^{\text{fail}} - L_n^{(0)} \rangle$:
$$\mu < 1 \Rightarrow \text{decay}, \quad \mu \geq 1 \Rightarrow \text{propagation}$$

**Cascade Size Distribution (Key Result):**
The probability of a cascade of size $k$ decreases approximately as:
$$P(K = k) \approx \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^k}{k!}$$
(Poissonian) for **subcritical** cascades, with deviations from Poissonian to **power-law** near the critical point.

(7 marks: 2 for model definition + 2 for recursion + 3 for conditions)

**(b) Star Graph $K_{1,4}$ Numerical Analysis:**

**Step 1: Initial Betweenness (Load):**

In a star graph, the central node $c$ lies on **all** paths between leaves. For a pair $(l_i, l_j)$, the path is $l_i - c - l_j$, so $c$ lies on the path. Number of leaf pairs: $\binom{4}{2} = 6$.

- $B_c = 6$ (all 6 pairs pass through $c$).
- $B_{l_i} = 0$ for each leaf (endpoints, no paths pass through).

**Step 2: Initial Capacity ($\alpha = 0.5$):**
- $C_c = 1.5 \times 6 = 9$
- $C_{l_i} = 0$ (for each leaf)

**Step 3: Remove Central Node $c$.**

Network becomes 4 isolated nodes. Each leaf has no neighbors. $B_{l_i}' = 0$.

**Step 4: Check Failure Condition:**
- $B_{l_i}' = 0 \leq 0 = C_{l_i}$ ✓ (trivially, since both are 0)
- No further failures.

**Step 5: Critical $\alpha$ for Central-Node Removal:**

After removing $c$, the network has no edges. To avoid further failures, we need:
$$B_{l_i}^{(1)} = 0 \leq C_{l_i} = (1+\alpha) \cdot B_{l_i}(0) = 0$$
This is always satisfied regardless of $\alpha$. Therefore, **any value of $\alpha$ prevents further cascade** because leaves have zero initial load.

**Step 6: More Insight — What if we remove a leaf?**

Removing $l_1$: network becomes $K_{1,3}$ with 3 leaves. The central node's betweenness drops from 6 to 3 (pairs among remaining leaves). 
- New $B_c' = 3 \leq C_c = 9$ ✓ → No cascade.

The star graph is **highly robust** to leaf removal, but **removal of the central node causes complete fragmentation** at the structural level (no edges left).

(7 marks: 2 for initial betweenness + 1 for capacity + 2 for cascade + 2 for $\alpha$ analysis)

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Cascading Failure Modeling: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Cascade Definition:** Sequential failure propagation triggered by redistribution of load after initial node/edge removal.
- ✅ **Three Ingredients:** Load definition + Capacity constraint + Redistribution rule.
- ✅ **Motter–Lai Model:** Load = betweenness centrality, capacity $C_i = (1+\alpha) L_i(0)$.
- ✅ **Critical Tolerance:** $\alpha_c = \max_i \frac{B_i^{(1)} - B_i}{B_i}$.
- ✅ **CASCADE Model:** Load recursion $L_{n+1} = L_n + \Delta P$ with random initial disturbance.
- ✅ **Branching Process:** $\mu < 1$ (subcritical, decay), $\mu = 1$ (critical, power-law), $\mu > 1$ (supercritical, exponential growth).
- ✅ **Interdependent Networks:** First-order (discontinuous) phase transition at $p_c \approx 2.4 / \langle k \rangle$.
- ✅ **Single Network Percolation:** Second-order (continuous) transition at $p_c = 1 / \langle k \rangle$.
- ✅ **Watts Threshold Model:** Global cascades possible even at $\theta \sim 0.18$ due to network clustering.
- ✅ **Global Efficiency:** $E[G] = \frac{1}{N(N-1)} \sum_{i \neq j} \frac{1}{d_{ij}}$.
- ✅ **Robustness Metric:** $R = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{Q=1}^N S(Q)$ (area under attack curve).
- ✅ **Scale-Free Robustness:** Random failure tolerance via $\alpha_c \sim 1/\log N$, but vulnerable to targeted hub attacks.
- ✅ **Real-World Domains:** Power grids, BGP internet, financial contagion, cyber-physical systems.
- ✅ **Engineering Goal:** Identify critical nodes, allocate capacity, design redundancy.
- ✅ **Always recompute** betweenness after each removal — a common KTU error is using initial loads.
- ✅ **State units** in every numerical answer (e.g., MW for power, packets for internet, transactions for finance).
- ✅ **Distinguish** cascade "extinction" (size < 5% of N) from "catastrophic cascade" (size > 50%).
- ✅ **Final state** is always an **absorbing state** where no node exceeds its capacity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Failure Propagation Model
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Failure Propagation Model

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

The **Failure Propagation Model (FPM)** is a class of deterministic or stochastic network models that mathematically describes how the failure of one or a few nodes/links initiates a cascade of subsequent failures across the network, ultimately leading to macroscopic breakdown, fragmentation, or collapse. It is studied under the broader framework of **complex network resilience analysis**, where the network is treated as a dynamical system whose state evolves under redistribution rules of load (flow) after the removal of elements.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, FPM encompasses:
- **Cascading Failures** (sequential, triggered by load redistribution).
- **Percolation-Based Failures** (random or targeted removal of nodes).
- **Threshold-Based Propagation** (epidemic-like, SIR-type spreading).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A *Failure Propagation Model* must define three components — **(i)** a *load* or *state variable* on every node/link, **(ii)** a *capacity* threshold beyond which the element fails, and **(iii)** a *redistribution rule* describing how load from a failed element is shared by its neighbors.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **traffic roundabout** where vehicles are the "load" and the connecting roads are the "nodes." If one road is suddenly closed (a failure), all incoming traffic is rerouted through the remaining roads. If those roads can only handle a limited number of cars (their *capacity*), an overload occurs, and they too shut down. The domino effect that follows — cars flowing from one blocked road to the next — is exactly what a *cascading failure propagation* does in a network.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Networks rarely collapse because of the original failure alone. They collapse because of the *redistributed load* that the surviving nodes are not *designed* to bear. The propagation is, therefore, a **second-order effect** triggered by the first-order perturbation.

### Standard Metrics Used in FPM (KTU Board-Expected Terminology)

- **Node Betweenness Centrality** $B_i$ — total number of shortest paths passing through node $i$.
- **Node Load** $L_i$ — flow (e.g., betweenness) handled by node $i$.
- **Node Capacity** $C_i$ — maximum flow node $i$ can sustain.
- **Cascade Size** $\sigma$ — fraction of nodes in the largest connected component after the cascade ends.
- **Tolerance Parameter** $\alpha$ — defines capacity margin as $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$.
- **Critical Tolerance** $\alpha_c$ — minimum $\alpha$ below which a cascade is system-wide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to write all three components (load, capacity, redistribution) explicitly when defining *any* failure propagation model. Omitting one of them is the most common reason for partial credit in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Foundational Axioms of Failure Propagation

Every FPM discussed under the KTU 2024 syllabus is built on three logical layers:

1. **Static Layer — The Network Itself**
   - Graph $G = (V, E)$ with $N = \vert V \vert$ nodes and $M = \vert E \vert$ edges.
   - A *load* function $L : V \cup E \rightarrow \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ is defined (usually betweenness, degree, or current flow).

2. **Threshold Layer — The Failure Criterion**
   - Each element has a *capacity* $C$ such that **failure occurs if and only if load exceeds capacity**, i.e., $L > C$.
   - Capacity is a *design parameter* that can be uniform, proportional to load, or degree-based.

3. **Dynamic Layer — The Redistribution Rule**
   - When element $i$ fails at time step $t$, its load $L_i$ is distributed to its neighbors according to a rule (equal-share, proportional-share, or global recomputation).
   - This redistribution may cause *new* elements to fail at $t+1$, creating an iterative cascade.

> [!NOTE]
> The cascade halts when no surviving element satisfies $L_j > C_j$. The number of elements lost in steady state is the **cascade size**, a key KTU evaluation metric.

### 2.2 Major FPM Variants Covered in KTU Module 3

#### A. Motter–Lai Cascading Failure Model (2002)
- Load on node $i$: $L_i = B_i$ (betweenness).
- Capacity of node $i$: $C_i = (1+\alpha)B_i$ where $\alpha \geq 0$.
- Redistribution: **Global** — shortest paths are recomputed on the *surviving* network.
- A node fails at step $t+1$ iff $L_i(t+1) > C_i$.

#### B. C&R (Coping with Random Failures) Local Redistribution Model
- Load $L_i = k_i$ (degree), or $L_i = $ local flow.
- Redistribution: **Local** — failed node's load is split among its $k$ surviving neighbors equally.
- $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$ with tolerance parameter $\alpha$.

#### C. Degree-Based Threshold Model (Watts 2002)
- Each node has a *threshold* $\phi_i \in [0,1]$.
- A node becomes inactive if more than $\phi_i k_i$ of its neighbors are inactive.
- Purely topological — does not require load recomputation.

#### D. Fiber-Bundle / Sandpile Model (Self-Organized Criticality)
- Continuous load accumulation until global redistribution "avalanches" occur.
- Captures the long-tail (power-law) distribution of cascade sizes.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Quantity | Symbol | Meaning / Engineering Use | Typical Units |
|---|----|----|----|----|
| 1 | Betweenness Centrality | $B_i = \sum_{s \neq t} \dfrac{\sigma_{st}(i)}{\sigma_{st}}$ | Measures how often a node lies on shortest paths — its "load" | dimensionless |
| 2 | Capacity (Motter–Lai) | $C_i = (1+\alpha)\,B_i$ | Hard-wired capacity of node $i$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Critical Tolerance | $\alpha_c = \dfrac{N}{N-1} - 1$ (for Motter–Lai on some graphs) | Minimum redundancy to stop global cascade | dimensionless |
| 4 | Failure Condition | $L_i(t) > C_i \Rightarrow$ node $i$ fails at step $t$ | Triggers propagation | — |
| 5 | Equal-Share Redistribution | $\Delta L_j = \dfrac{L_i^{\text{fail}}}{k_i^{\text{surv}}}$ | Load transferred to each surviving neighbor $j$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Proportional-Share Redistribution | $\Delta L_j = L_i^{\text{fail}} \cdot \dfrac{L_j}{\sum_{m \in \mathcal{N}(i)} L_m}$ | Heavier nodes get more transferred load | dimensionless |
| 7 | Cascade Size (steady state) | $\sigma = \dfrac{\vert V_{\text{survive}} \vert}{N}$ | Fraction of nodes that remain connected | dimensionless |
| 8 | Percolation Threshold | $p_c = \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$ | Random node removal probability at which the giant component vanishes (ER network) | dimensionless |
| 9 | SIR Cascade Rate | $\dfrac{dR}{dt} = \beta\,S\,I$ | Epidemic-style propagation rate | 1/time |
| 10 | Avalanche Size (sandpile) | $P(s) \sim s^{-\tau}$ | Power-law cascade-size distribution | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When writing these equations in your KTU answer script, always **state the redistribution rule explicitly**. A formula without its rule is treated as incomplete by the examiner and can lead to loss of up to **2 marks** out of 14.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power Grids** — prevent blackouts by sizing transmission line capacity using Motter–Lai-style $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$ engineering standards.
- **Internet / Routing** — BGP route redistribution cascading failures (e.g., 2008 YouTube outage).
- **Supply-Chain Networks** — local supplier failure propagates to dependent firms.
- **Air-Traffic / Road Networks** — capacity-aware rerouting prevents gridlock cascades.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Critical Tolerance $\alpha_c$ in the Motter–Lai Model

The Motter–Lai model assumes $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$ where $L_i$ is the *initial* betweenness. After one node failure, betweenness is recomputed on the surviving subgraph. The cascade is *system-wide* iff the load on at least one surviving neighbor exceeds its capacity.

We aim to derive a closed-form bound for the **minimum** $\alpha$ that prevents global cascade on a *homogeneous* random (ER) network with mean degree $\langle k \rangle$.

**Step 1: Define initial betweenness.**
On an ER graph with $N$ nodes and mean degree $\langle k \rangle$, the betweenness centrality of a typical node is approximately:
$$B_i \approx \frac{N(N-1)}{2 \cdot \langle k \rangle N} = \frac{N-1}{2}$$

**Explanation:** The number of node pairs is $\binom{N}{2} \approx N^2/2$. The average path length scales as $\ln N / \ln \langle k \rangle$, and the load per path is split among $\langle k \rangle$ nodes. A more standard mean-field expression is:
$$B_i \approx \frac{N}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 2: Capacity of a typical node.**
$$C_i = (1+\alpha)\,B_i = (1+\alpha)\frac{N}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 3: Maximum extra load a node can absorb before failing.**
$$\Delta_{\max} = C_i - B_i = \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 4: Compute extra load after a single node is removed.**
When a node $v$ of degree $k_v$ is removed, the paths that previously used $v$ must be rerouted. The extra load falling on its neighbor $j$ is, in the equal-share approximation:
$$\Delta L_j = \frac{B_v}{k_v^{\text{surv}}}$$

For an ER network, $k_v \approx \langle k \rangle$ and approximately half the failed node's betweenness is absorbed by the surviving neighbors:
$$\Delta L_j \approx \frac{B_v}{2 \langle k \rangle} \approx \frac{N}{2\langle k \rangle^{2}}$$

**Step 5: Set the failure criterion for the surviving node.**
The cascade stops at first order iff:
$$\Delta L_j \leq \Delta_{\max}$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{N}{2\langle k \rangle^{2}} \leq \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 6: Solve for the critical tolerance.**
$$\alpha \geq \frac{1}{2\langle k \rangle}$$

Hence the **critical tolerance** is:
$$\alpha_c = \frac{1}{2\langle k \rangle}$$

**Explanation:** The larger the mean degree, the smaller the redundancy margin required, because a single failure is absorbed by many neighbors.

> [!NOTE]
> On a *scale-free* network with exponent $\gamma = 3$, Motter & Lai showed empirically that $\alpha_c$ is significantly *higher* than the ER value, because hubs concentrate betweenness and are catastrophic single points of failure.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Percolation Threshold $p_c$ for Random Node Removal

**Step 1: Generating function for ER network degree distribution.**
The degree distribution is Poisson:
$$P(k) = e^{-\langle k \rangle}\frac{\langle k \rangle^{k}}{k!}$$

The corresponding generating function is:
$$G_0(x) = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} P(k)\,x^{k} = e^{\langle k \rangle(x-1)}$$

**Step 2: Probability of a randomly chosen edge leading to a non-removed node.**
If a fraction $p$ of nodes survive, then the *excess* degree (degree of a node reached by following a random edge) conditioned on survival is:
$$\tilde{q} = p \cdot \frac{G_0'(1)}{G_0'(1)} = p$$

But for the *giant component* in the surviving subgraph, we trace a branch of edges until it terminates. The probability $u$ that the branch *does not* lead to the giant component satisfies:
$$u = 1 - p + p\,G_1(u)$$

where $G_1(x) = G_0'(x)/G_0'(1)$ is the generating function of the excess degree distribution.

For ER: $G_1(x) = G_0(x)$, hence:
$$u = 1 - p + p\,e^{\langle k \rangle(u-1)}$$

**Step 3: Giant component exists iff $u < 1$.**
Linearizing around $u=1$:
$$u \approx 1 - p\,\langle k \rangle (u-1) + O((u-1)^2)$$

Setting $u = 1 - \epsilon$:
$$1 - \epsilon = 1 - p - p\,\langle k \rangle(-\epsilon)$$
$$\epsilon = p + p\,\langle k \rangle\,\epsilon$$
$$\epsilon\,(1 - p\,\langle k \rangle) = p$$
$$\epsilon = \frac{p}{1 - p\,\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 4: Percolation threshold.**
The denominator becomes zero when $p\,\langle k \rangle = 1$, i.e.:
$$p_c = \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Explanation:** Below $p_c$, no giant component exists; the network fragments into many small clusters. This is the *random failure* counterpart to targeted attacks on hubs, which give a much larger $p_c$ and hence more fragile networks.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Question:** Consider a star network with a central hub of degree $k_h = 5$ and 5 leaves. The hub is assigned capacity $C_h = (1+\alpha)L_h$ with $L_h = 1.0$ (normalized betweenness). The leaves each carry $L_l = 0.05$. If the hub fails, by what fraction does each surviving leaf's load increase? Find the minimum $\alpha$ for no cascading failure.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Load of hub $L_h = 1.0$.** Total network load is conserved; sum of all leaf loads = $5 \times 0.05 = 0.25$. The hub handles the cross-leaf traffic, so $L_h = 1.0$ by normalization.

**Step 2: Redistribution.** Upon hub failure, the 1.0 unit of load is split equally among the 5 leaves:
$$\Delta L_l = \frac{1.0}{5} = 0.20$$

**Step 3: New load on each leaf.**
$$L_l^{\text{new}} = 0.05 + 0.20 = 0.25$$

**Step 4: Capacity of each leaf (Motter–Lai).**
$$C_l = (1+\alpha) \times 0.05 = 0.05 + 0.05\alpha$$

**Step 5: Failure condition $L_l^{\text{new}} > C_l$.**
$$0.25 > 0.05 + 0.05\alpha$$
$$0.20 > 0.05\alpha$$
$$\alpha < 4$$

**Step 6: Minimum $\alpha$ for no cascade.**
$$\boxed{\alpha_{\min} = 4}$$

**Interpretation:** Each leaf must be over-engineered by **400%** of its original load to absorb the hub's redistributed traffic. This illustrates the *fragility of centralized topologies* — a well-known KTU-examined result.

> [!TIP]
> Always show **Step 4** (capacity expression) clearly. Examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for stating the capacity rule.

---

### 3.4 Summary of Analytical Workflow Used in KTU 14-Mark Answers

| Step | Activity | Marks (typical) |
|------|----------|-------|
| 1 | Define load, capacity, redistribution rule | 3 |
| 2 | Set up the governing equation for $L_i$ or $\Delta L$ | 3 |
| 3 | Compute numerical example or symbolic bound | 4 |
| 4 | Identify critical parameter (e.g., $\alpha_c$, $p_c$) | 2 |
| 5 | Interpret result in real-world context | 2 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Failure Propagation Model

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **modular architecture** of a typical FPM simulation pipeline, mapping the flow of data between the static network layer, the dynamic cascade engine, and the resilience evaluator.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Input: Network Graph G] --> nodeB[Load Assignment Module]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Capacity Assignment Module]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Initial State: All nodes ACTIVE]
    nodeD --> nodeE{Trigger: Initial Failure}
    nodeE --> nodeF[Redistribution Engine]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Load vs Capacity Check}
    nodeG -->|L exceeds C| nodeH[Mark Node as FAILED]
    nodeG -->|L within C| nodeI[Node SURVIVES]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Update Network Topology]
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ --> nodeK{Any New Failure?}
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeF
    nodeK -->|No| nodeL[Compute Cascade Size sigma]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Resilience Metrics Output]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Output: alpha_c, sigma, p_c]

    style nodeA fill:#1f77b4,color:#fff
    style nodeN fill:#2ca02c,color:#fff
    style nodeE fill:#ff7f0e,color:#fff
    style nodeG fill:#d62728,color:#fff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **blue** block (top) is the static input stage.
- The **orange** block is the perturbation event (e.g., a single node removal).
- The **red** diamond is the per-step failure criterion.
- The loop `nodeF → nodeG → nodeH → nodeJ → nodeK → nodeF` represents the *iterative cascade time steps*.
- The **green** block (bottom) terminates the cascade and produces key KTU-board-expected outputs: critical tolerance $\alpha_c$ and cascade size $\sigma$.

---

### 4.2 Cascade Propagation Topology Diagram

This diagram illustrates how a *single-node failure* in a heterogeneous scale-free network (hubs highlighted) creates *secondary* and *tertiary* failures, exactly as defined in the Motter–Lai framework.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hub1((HUB-1)) --- leaf1((a))
    hub1 --- leaf2((b))
    hub1 --- leaf3((c))
    hub1 --- hub2((HUB-2))
    hub1 --- hub3((HUB-3))
    hub2 --- leaf4((d))
    hub2 --- leaf5((e))
    hub2 --- leaf6((f))
    hub3 --- leaf7((g))
    hub3 --- leaf8((h))
    hub3 --- leaf9((i))

    hub1 -.X.-> hub1
    hub1 --> leaf1
    hub1 --> leaf2
    leaf1 -.X.-> leaf1
    leaf2 -.X.-> leaf2

    classDef hub fill:#ff6b6b,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#4ecdc4,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    classDef failed fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px

    class hub1,hub2,hub3 hub
    class leaf1,leaf2,leaf3,leaf4,leaf5,leaf6,leaf7,leaf8,leaf9 leaf
```

**Interpretation:**
- **Red nodes** = Hubs (high betweenness).
- **Teal nodes** = Leaves (low betweenness).
- **Black X labels** = nodes that have crossed their capacity threshold and failed.
- Solid arrows from `HUB-1` to `a`, `b` show load *redistribution* after `HUB-1` fails.
- Leaves `a`, `b`, `c` become overloaded and *also* fail (their capacity $C_a = (1+\alpha)L_a$ is exceeded by the redistributed traffic).
- This is a **first-order cascade**; tertiary failures occur if $\alpha$ is too small.

---

### 4.3 State-Transition Flow of a Node

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ACTIVE
    ACTIVE --> OVERLOADED: Received Delta L
    OVERLOADED --> FAILED: L exceeds C
    OVERLOADED --> ACTIVE: L within C
    FAILED --> [*]: Removed from network
    ACTIVE --> FAILED: External attack
```

**Reading the Diagram:** Every node in the FPM has three operational states — `ACTIVE`, `OVERLOADED`, and `FAILED`. Transitions are governed by the load-capacity inequality, and a failed node is permanently removed (matches the *absorbing state* concept in stochastic process theory).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
Define the **Failure Propagation Model (FPM)**. List its three essential components.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A Failure Propagation Model is a mathematical framework used in network science to describe how the failure of one or more nodes/edges in a network triggers a *cascade* of further failures. Its three essential components are:
1. **Load** $L_i$ on each node/link (e.g., betweenness, degree, current flow).
2. **Capacity** $C_i$ — the maximum load a node can sustain without failing.
3. **Redistribution Rule** — the mechanism by which the load of a failed element is reallocated to surviving elements (equal-share, proportional-share, or global recomputation).

A node $i$ fails whenever $L_i > C_i$, and the cascade propagates iteratively until the network reaches a stable state.

---

#### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
What is the **critical tolerance parameter** $\alpha_c$ in the Motter–Lai cascading failure model? Why is $\alpha_c$ larger for scale-free networks than for random ER networks?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The critical tolerance $\alpha_c$ is the **minimum** value of the tolerance parameter $\alpha$ (defined via $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$) that is sufficient to **prevent** a global cascade following a single-node failure. For ER networks, $\alpha_c \approx 1/(2\langle k \rangle)$.

For **scale-free** networks, $\alpha_c$ is *significantly larger* because betweenness centrality is **heterogeneously distributed** — a few hub nodes carry a disproportionately large share of the shortest paths. When a hub fails, its enormous load is dumped onto a small set of neighbors, demanding a much higher capacity margin to absorb. Hence the cascade is harder to suppress.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> Internal choice: Answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Derive the **percolation threshold** $p_c = 1/\langle k \rangle$ for random node removal in an Erdős–Rényi (ER) random graph of mean degree $\langle k \rangle$. Explain why this result implies that ER networks are *robust* to random failures. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider a 6-node line network (nodes labeled 1–2–3–4–5–6 in a chain). Each edge carries unit flow between its two endpoints, and node load is defined as $L_i =$ number of shortest paths through node $i$. A capacity $\alpha$-rule $C_i = (1+\alpha)L_i$ is used. **(i)** Compute the initial load $L_i$ of every node. **(ii)** If node 3 fails and equal-share redistribution occurs, compute the new loads. **(iii)** Find the minimum $\alpha$ to prevent any further failures. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** The degree distribution of an ER network with mean degree $\langle k \rangle$ is Poisson:
$$P(k) = e^{-\langle k \rangle} \frac{\langle k \rangle^{k}}{k!}$$

**Step 2:** The generating function is:
$$G_0(x) = e^{\langle k \rangle(x-1)}$$
$$G_1(x) = G_0(x) \quad \text{(ER networks are degree-uncorrelated)}$$

**Step 3:** Let $u$ be the probability that following a randomly chosen edge does *not* lead to the giant component. The self-consistent equation is:
$$u = 1 - p + p\,G_1(u) = 1 - p + p\,e^{\langle k \rangle(u-1)}$$

**Step 4:** Linearize around $u = 1$. Setting $u = 1 - \epsilon$ with small $\epsilon$:
$$1 - \epsilon = 1 - p + p\,e^{-\langle k \rangle \epsilon}$$
$$1 - \epsilon \approx 1 - p - p\,\langle k \rangle\,\epsilon$$
$$\epsilon = p + p\,\langle k \rangle\,\epsilon$$
$$\epsilon(1 - p\,\langle k \rangle) = p$$
$$\epsilon = \frac{p}{1 - p\,\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 5:** A *giant component* exists only if $\epsilon > 0$ and finite, i.e., $1 - p\,\langle k \rangle > 0$, which gives the critical threshold:
$$\boxed{p_c = \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle}}$$

**Interpretation (2 marks):** For large ER networks with $\langle k \rangle \gg 1$, $p_c$ is very small, meaning even after removing a large fraction $1 - p$ of nodes at random, a giant connected component still survives. This is the *robustness of random networks to random failure* — a hallmark result in network science (Albert, Jeong, Barabási 2000).

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**(i) Initial loads (3 marks):**

The unique shortest path between any two nodes on a line is the line itself. So node $i$ lies on the shortest path between any pair $(s,t)$ that "straddles" it. The number of such pairs for node $i$ on a line of 6 nodes is:
$$L_i = i \cdot (6-i)$$

- $L_1 = 1 \cdot 5 = 5$ — wait, betweenness in undirected graphs counts each unordered pair once. The number of unordered pairs straddling node $i$ in a path of 6 nodes is $i(N-i)$. But for undirected betweenness we divide by 2:
$$L_i = \frac{i(N-i)}{2}, \quad N = 6$$

So:
- $L_1 = 1 \times 5 / 2 = 2.5$
- $L_2 = 2 \times 4 / 2 = 4.0$
- $L_3 = 3 \times 3 / 2 = 4.5$
- $L_4 = 4 \times 2 / 2 = 4.0$
- $L_5 = 5 \times 1 / 2 = 2.5$
- $L_6 = 6 \times 0 / 2 = 0.0$

**(ii) After node 3 fails (2 marks):**
The line breaks into two segments: $\{1, 2\}$ and $\{4, 5, 6\}$. There are no paths between the two segments, so all cross-segment traffic is lost. Within each segment, recompute betweenness:
- Segment A (nodes 1, 2): $L_1^{A} = 0, L_2^{A} = 0$ (only one pair, 1-2, and both are endpoints of the segment; node 2 is an internal point only for pairs within the segment, but here there is only one pair (1,2), so node 2 is on the path. Actually, for segment A: $L_2^A = 1 \times 0 / 2 = 0$?)

Re-evaluation: For segment A = {1, 2}, the only pair is (1, 2). Node 1 is an endpoint of the path; node 2 is also an endpoint. *No node lies strictly between them.* So $L_1^A = L_2^A = 0$ by the standard betweenness definition (endpoints do not count).

- Segment B (nodes 4, 5, 6): only the pair (4, 6) and the path 4-5-6. Node 5 is *on* this path. So $L_5^B = 1$ (one path through it), and $L_4^B = L_6^B = 0$.

**Final loads after node 3's failure:**
- $L_1 = 0, \ L_2 = 0, \ L_4 = 0, \ L_5 = 1, \ L_6 = 0$

**(iii) Minimum $\alpha$ (2 marks):**
The only node that has *increased* its load is node 5, which jumped from 4.0 to 1.0 — actually, the load *decreased*! Hence **no node exceeds its capacity**, and **any** $\alpha \geq 0$ suffices:
$$\boxed{\alpha_{\min} = 0}$$

**Insight (for interpretation marks):** This is a counter-intuitive case — a line graph is so sparsely connected that the failure of an internal node actually *fragments* the network rather than redistributing load, because there is no alternative path. The cascade terminates immediately. This is the *pathological* limit of low-connectivity topologies.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the **Motter–Lai cascading failure model** in detail. Define the tolerance parameter $\alpha$ and derive the expression for the critical tolerance $\alpha_c \approx 1/(2\langle k \rangle)$ for an ER network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-node square network has nodes {1, 2, 3, 4} with edges (1-2), (2-3), (3-4), (4-1), (1-3). Betweenness centrality values are: $B_1 = 3.0, B_2 = 1.0, B_3 = 3.0, B_4 = 1.0$. Capacities follow $C_i = (1+\alpha)B_i$. **(i)** What is the maximum tolerable $\alpha$ below which a cascade occurs when node 1 is initially removed? **(ii)** If $\alpha = 0.1$ is chosen, identify the *order* (number of steps) of the cascade. **(7 marks)**

##### **Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 (Model definition — 2 marks):** The Motter–Lai model defines:
- **Load** of node $i$: $L_i = B_i$ (betweenness centrality, computed initially).
- **Capacity** of node $i$: $C_i = (1+\alpha)B_i$ with $\alpha \geq 0$.
- **Failure condition**: $L_i(t) > C_i$ at any time step $t$.
- **Redistribution**: After each failure, *global* shortest paths are recomputed on the surviving subgraph, which can drastically increase betweenness on remaining nodes.

**Step 2 (Derivation — 4 marks):** *(Reproduce the derivation in Section 3.1 verbatim, but the key steps are:)*
- Initial betweenness per node: $B_i \approx N/\langle k \rangle$ (ER network).
- Capacity margin: $\Delta_{\max} = \alpha B_i$.
- Extra load on a surviving neighbor after one node removal: $\Delta L_j \approx B_v / (2\langle k \rangle) \approx N/(2\langle k \rangle^2)$.
- Setting $\Delta L_j \leq \Delta_{\max}$:
$$\frac{N}{2\langle k \rangle^2} \leq \alpha \cdot \frac{N}{\langle k \rangle} \Rightarrow \alpha_c = \frac{1}{2\langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 3 (Interpretation — 1 mark):** For ER networks, larger mean degree $\Rightarrow$ lower $\alpha_c$ required. For scale-free networks with hub concentration, $\alpha_c$ is empirically *larger*, indicating greater fragility.

---

##### **Solution to (b):**

**(i) Maximum tolerable $\alpha$ (3 marks):**

When node 1 fails, recompute betweenness on the surviving 3-node graph: {2, 3, 4} with edges (2-3), (3-4). The pair (2, 4) has only one shortest path: 2-3-4, of length 2. The pair (2, 3) has path 2-3 (length 1). The pair (3, 4) has path 3-4 (length 1).

- $B_2^{\text{new}} = 1$ (the path 2-3-4 passes through node 2? No, it goes 2-3-4. Node 2 is an *endpoint* of this path. By convention, endpoints do not contribute to betweenness.)
- $B_3^{\text{new}} = 1$ (the only path through node 3 strictly is 2-3-4, so $B_3 = 1$).
- $B_4^{\text{new}} = 0$ similarly.

Comparing:
- Node 2: new $B_2 = 1 \leq C_2 = (1+\alpha)(1.0) = 1 + \alpha$ — always satisfied for $\alpha \geq 0$.
- Node 3: new $B_3 = 1 \leq C_3 = (1+\alpha)(3.0) = 3 + 3\alpha$ — always satisfied.
- Node 4: new $B_4 = 0 \leq C_4 = (1+\alpha)(1.0) = 1 + \alpha$ — always satisfied.

**Result:** No node exceeds its capacity, so cascade is trivial for any $\alpha \geq 0$. So the *maximum tolerable $\alpha$ below which a cascade occurs* is technically $\alpha_c = 0$ (i.e., cascade *never* occurs in this particular graph for this particular initial failure).

**Caveat:** This is a small-graph artifact. For larger scale-free motifs, the answer would be non-zero.

**(ii) Cascade order at $\alpha = 0.1$ (4 marks):**

Since no node fails in step 1 (as shown above), the cascade order is **zero** — the perturbation has no propagation. All nodes survive, and the cascade size is $\sigma = 1$ (all 3 remaining nodes remain connected to a sufficient extent, though node 4 is a leaf).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget that **endpoints of a path do not contribute** to betweenness centrality. A node must be *strictly between* two other nodes to register betweenness. Forgetting this costs 1–2 marks in many KTU solutions.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Students may not specify whether the redistribution is *local* or *global* in the Motter–Lai model. The Motter–Lai model uses **global** recomputation, while the C&R model uses **local** equal-share. Conflating them costs 2 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** For cascade questions, students sometimes forget to **iterate** the failure check. A cascade may require 2, 3, or more time steps. Always recompute betweenness (or redistributed load) *after* every wave of failures.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Not stating $L_i > C_i$ vs $L_i \geq C_i$ convention — the standard in the KTU 2024 scheme is **strict** inequality.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Failure Propagation Model**

- ✅ A Failure Propagation Model is defined by **three pillars**: *load*, *capacity*, and *redistribution rule*.
- ✅ Failure occurs when **$L_i > C_i$** (strict inequality).
- ✅ **Motter–Lai** uses $C_i = (1+\alpha)B_i$ and **global** betweenness recomputation.
- ✅ **C&R (Local Redistribution)** uses equal-share local transfer to surviving neighbors.
- ✅ **Critical tolerance** for ER network (Motter–Lai): $\alpha_c = 1/(2\langle k \rangle)$.
- ✅ **Percolation threshold** for random node removal in ER graph: $p_c = 1/\langle k \rangle$.
- ✅ **Scale-free** networks are *robust* to random failure but *fragile* to targeted hub attack.
- ✅ **Betweenness centrality** for node $i$ in a path of $N$ nodes: $B_i = i(N-i)/2$ (undirected).
- ✅ Always specify *which* node is initially removed and *which* redistribution rule is applied.
- ✅ Cascade **size** $\sigma$ is measured in steady state; cascade **order** is the number of *time steps* to reach steady state.
- ✅ Real-world analogues: power grids, BGP routing, supply chains, and pandemic spreading.
- ✅ For SIR-type epidemic propagation, the basic reproduction number is $R_0 = \beta/\gamma$, and a pandemic emerges iff $R_0 > 1$.
- ✅ Sandpile / self-organized criticality models produce **power-law** cascade-size distributions $P(s) \sim s^{-\tau}$.
- ✅ Remember the **physical intuition**: cascades are *second-order* effects caused by load redistribution, not by the original failure itself.

> [!TIP]
> For 14-mark answers, the structure that maximizes marks is: **(1) State all three model components explicitly** → **(2) Write the governing equation(s)** → **(3) Show the numerical/symbolic working** → **(4) Identify the critical parameter** → **(5) Provide real-world interpretation**. Examiners reward this exact flow.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Branching Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Branching Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Branching Process (Galton–Watson Process):** A stochastic discrete-time Markov process $(X_n)_{n \ge 0}$ that models the evolution of a population in which each individual in generation $n$ independently produces a random number of offspring belonging to generation $n+1$, with the *same* offspring distribution.

- Let $\xi$ denote the number of offspring produced by a *typical* individual, with probability mass function
$$P(\xi = k) = p_k, \qquad k = 0, 1, 2, \dots$$
- $X_n$ = total number of individuals in generation $n$ (the **branching random variable**).
- $X_0 = 1$ (a single founding ancestor).
- The recursion is
$$X_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{X_n} \xi_i^{(n)}$$
where $\{\xi_i^{(n)}\}$ are i.i.d. copies of $\xi$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — Rumour / Disease Spread:** Imagine you are patient zero in a village. Each person you meet either catches the rumour (and passes it to others) or doesn't. Each newly "infected" person then independently spreads it to others in the next generation. The **branching model** captures exactly this cascade: a *tree* of infections where every node produces a random number of children. The key question is — **will the rumour die out or take over the village?**

The branching model is the *mathematical engine* behind epidemic theory, viral marketing, rumour cascades, cascading failures in power grids, and even phylogenetic trees in biology.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlight

- **Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$:** mean number of offspring of one individual, $R_0 = E[\xi]$.
- **Extinction Probability $q$:** probability that the process eventually contains 0 individuals.
- **Probability Generating Function (PGF):** $G(s) = \sum_k p_k s^k$.
- **Critical threshold:** $R_0 = 1$ separates extinction-always from survival-possible regimes.
- **Epidemic threshold on networks:** transmissibility $T_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 - k \rangle$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Branching Model

- **Step 1 — Founding:** Begin with $X_0 = 1$ ancestor.
- **Step 2 — Reproduction:** Each individual of generation $n$ produces $\xi \sim \{p_k\}$ i.i.d. offspring, forming generation $n+1$.
- **Step 3 — Recursion:** $X_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{X_n} \xi_i^{(n)}$. The conditional distribution is
$$P(X_{n+1} = m \mid X_n = k) = P\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^k \xi_i = m\right).$$
- **Step 4 — Termination:** A generation may produce $k=0$ children ⇒ the branch dies; if every branch dies ⇒ global extinction.
- **Step 5 — Threshold behaviour:**
  - If $R_0 < 1$ : **sub-critical** ⇒ extinction with probability **1**.
  - If $R_0 = 1$ : **critical** ⇒ extinction with probability **1** (unless $p_1 = 1$).
  - If $R_0 > 1$ : **super-critical** ⇒ extinction prob $q < 1$; survival prob $1 - q > 0$.

## 2.2 Probability Generating Function — Why?

Because $X_{n+1}$ is a sum of $X_n$ i.i.d. variables, PGFs turn the convolution into multiplication:
$$G_{X_{n+1}}(s) = G_{X_n}\bigl(G(s)\bigr) = G^{\circ (n+1)}(s).$$
This means the **$n$-th generation PGF is the $(n+1)$-fold composition** $G^{\circ(n+1)}$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| Quantity | Formula | Notation / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Offspring PMF | $p_k = P(\xi=k)$ | dimensionless |
| Probability Generating Function (PGF) | $G(s) = \sum_{k=0}^\infty p_k\, s^k$ | $s \in [0,1]$ |
| Mean offspring (Basic Reproduction No.) | $R_0 = G'(1) = \sum_k k\, p_k$ | dimensionless |
| Variance of offspring | $\sigma^2 = G''(1) + G'(1) - \bigl(G'(1)\bigr)^2$ | dimensionless |
| Generation recursion (PGF) | $G_{X_{n+1}}(s) = G_{X_n}(G(s))$ | — |
| Extinction probability equation | $q = G(q)$ | smallest root in $[0,1]$ |
| Survival probability | $1 - q$ | — |
| Critical threshold | $R_0 = 1$ | — |
| Epidemic threshold on network | $T_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 - k \rangle$ | transmissibility |
| Transmissibility (SIR) | $T = \beta / \gamma$ | probability per contact |
| Multi-type PGF | $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{s}) = \sum_{\mathbf{k}} p_{\mathbf{k}}\, \mathbf{s}^{\mathbf{k}}$ | vector form |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always** solve $q = G(q)$ by inspection first: $q = 1$ is **always** a fixed point. The extinction probability is the **smallest non-negative root**.

## 2.4 Engineering & Real-World Utility

- **Epidemiology:** Approximate the early phase of an SIR outbreak before the depletion of susceptibles bends the tree.
- **Computer Virus Propagation:** Estimate the likelihood that a worm reaches a critical mass.
- **Power-Grid Cascades:** Each failing line trips downstream lines (branching cascade).
- **Marketing & Social Networks:** Predict whether a piece of viral content "takes off."
- **Phylogenetics:** Reconstruct ancestral trees of species / mutations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean of $X_n$ (Tree Growth)

**Claim:** $E[X_n] = R_0^{\,n}$.

Starting from the recursion $X_{n+1} = \sum_{i=1}^{X_n} \xi_i^{(n)}$, we condition on $X_n$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E[X_{n+1}] &= E\!\left[E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{X_n} \xi_i^{(n)} \,\Big|\, X_n\right]\right] \\
&= E\!\left[X_n \cdot E[\xi]\right] \quad (\text{the }\xi_i \text{ are i.i.d. with mean } R_0) \\
&= R_0 \cdot E[X_n].
\end{aligned}
$$

Iterating with $E[X_0] = 1$ gives
$$E[X_n] = R_0^{\,n}.$$

**Interpretation:** The population grows exponentially when $R_0>1$, decays exponentially when $R_0<1$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Variance of $X_n$

**Claim:** $\text{Var}(X_n) = \sigma^2 R_0^{\,n-1}\dfrac{R_0^{\,n}-1}{R_0-1}$ for $R_0 \neq 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Var}(X_{n+1}) &= E\!\left[\text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{X_n}\xi_i \,\Big|\, X_n\right)\right] + \text{Var}\!\left(E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{X_n}\xi_i \,\Big|\, X_n\right]\right) \\
&= E\!\left[X_n \sigma^2\right] + \text{Var}\!\left(X_n R_0\right) \\
&= \sigma^2 R_0^{\,n} + R_0^2 \,\text{Var}(X_n).
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving this linear recurrence yields the claim (induction on $n$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Extinction Probability Equation

Let $q_n = P(X_n = 0)$. By the tower rule and independence across branches:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{n+1} &= P(X_{n+1}=0) = \sum_{k=0}^\infty P(X_n=k)\,P(X_{k+1}^{(k)}=0) \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^\infty P(X_n=k)\,\bigl[G(0)\bigr]^k \quad\text{(each of the }k\text{ sub-trees must die)} \\
&= E\!\left[G(0)^{X_n}\right] = G_{X_n}\!\bigl(G(0)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n \to \infty$, $X_n \to 0$ with probability $q$ (extinction) and $X_n \to \infty$ with prob $1-q$. Under continuity, $q = G(q)$.

Solving $q = G(q)$ for the **Geometric** offspring distribution $p_k = (1-p)\,p^{k-1}$ for $k\ge 1$ and $p_0 = 1-p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) &= (1-p) + \sum_{k=1}^\infty (1-p)p^{k-1} s^k \\
&= (1-p) + (1-p)\,p\,\sum_{k=1}^\infty (ps)^{k-1} s \\
&= (1-p) + \frac{(1-p)\,p\,s}{1-ps} \\
&= \frac{1-p}{1-ps}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Set $q = G(q)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q &= \frac{1-p}{1-pq} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; q(1-pq) = 1-p \\
&\Longrightarrow q - pq^2 = 1 - p \\
&\Longrightarrow pq^2 - q + (1-p) = 0 \\
&\Longrightarrow q = \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - 4p(1-p)}}{2p} = \frac{1 - \vert 1-2p\vert}{2p}.
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $R_0 = 1/p$ for geometric, $R_0 > 1 \Leftrightarrow p < 1/2$ and the smaller root is

$$
q = \begin{cases} 1, & p \ge \tfrac12 \;(R_0 \le 1) \\ \dfrac{1-p}{p} = \dfrac{1}{R_0}, & p < \tfrac12 \;(R_0 > 1) \end{cases}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Branching Approximation for SIR on Networks

Consider SIR on a configuration-model graph. In the **early phase**, susceptible depletion is negligible, so each infected edge independently transmits with probability $T = \beta/\gamma$. The number of new infections along a *typical* edge follows a Poisson-binomial-like distribution, which we approximate as Poisson with mean

$$R_0 = T \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 - k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} = T \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}.$$

Here $\langle k^2 - k \rangle / \langle k \rangle$ is the **mean excess degree** — the expected number of *other* edges a node reached by a random edge has. Setting $R_0 = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 - k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} &= 1 \\
\Rightarrow \quad T_c &= \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 - k \rangle} = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2\rangle - \langle k\rangle}.
\end{aligned}
$$

- If $T < T_c$ ⇒ outbreak dies out exponentially.
- If $T > T_c$ ⇒ a **giant epidemic component** emerges (size $\sim N\cdot S$, with $S$ determined by percolation theory).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **ER random graph** $G(N, p)$ with mean degree $c$, $\langle k \rangle = c$ and $\langle k^2 \rangle = c + c^2$, giving
> $$T_c = \frac{c}{c^2} = \frac{1}{c}.$$
> This recovers the classic **epidemic threshold** $T_c = 1/c$ of homogeneous networks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Branching Process Generational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Generation 0: X0 = 1 ancestor"] --> B["Generation 1: each xi produces offspring"]
    B --> C["Xi drawn from PMF pk"]
    C --> D["Sum of offspring = X1 = sum xi"]
    D --> E["Check X1 > 0 ?"]
    E -- "No" --> F["Process Extinct, q achieved"]
    E -- "Yes" --> G["Generation 2 begins"]
    G --> H["Repeat sampling for every node"]
    H --> I{"R0 vs 1"}
    I -- "R0 < 1" --> J["Subcritical, q = 1"]
    I -- "R0 = 1" --> K["Critical, q = 1"]
    I -- "R0 > 1" --> L["Supercritical, q < 1 with survival 1 minus q"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Branching Approximation on Networks

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Network Setup"]
        N1["Configuration Model G with degree dist pk"]
    end
    subgraph S2["Per-Edge Branching"]
        N2["Transmissibility T = beta over gamma"]
        N3["Mean excess degree = (k2 minus k) over k"]
        N4["R0 = T times mean excess degree"]
    end
    subgraph S3["Threshold"]
        N5{"Compare R0 vs 1"}
    end
    subgraph S4["Outcome"]
        N6["Outbreak dies out"]
        N7["Giant epidemic emerges, size N times S"]
    end
    N1 --> N2
    N1 --> N3
    N2 --> N4
    N3 --> N4
    N4 --> N5
    N5 -- "R0 less than 1" --> N6
    N5 -- "R0 greater than 1" --> N7
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How Branching Theory Maps to Network Cascades

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Real Cascade Event on Network"] --> B["Network Topology G(N,L)"]
    B --> C["Degree Distribution pk, Excess Degree"]
    C --> D["Branching Tree Approximation"]
    D --> E["Offspring Distribution approx Poisson with mean R0"]
    E --> F["PGF G of s"]
    F --> G["Fixed-Point Equation q equals G of q"]
    G --> H["Extinction Probability q"]
    H --> I["Epidemic Threshold T_c"]
    I --> J["Decision: Outbreak or Die-out"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why block diagrams instead of physical drawings?** Branching processes are inherently *tree-structured cascades*. A block schematic cleanly shows the *cascade pipeline* (network → branching approximation → PGF → threshold) that KTU examiners expect students to label and annotate.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define a Galton–Watson branching process. State the condition for the process to die out with probability one.**

**Model Answer:**

A Galton–Watson branching process is a discrete-time stochastic process $\{X_n\}_{n\ge 0}$ in which each individual of generation $n$ independently gives rise to a random number $\xi$ of offspring in generation $n+1$, all with the **same offspring distribution** $P(\xi = k) = p_k$.

If $R_0 = E[\xi] = \sum_k k\,p_k \le 1$, then the extinction probability $q = 1$, i.e., the process dies out with probability one. Conversely, if $R_0 > 1$, the probability of *eventual* extinction $q < 1$ and is the unique root in $(0,1)$ of $q = G(q) = \sum_k p_k q^k$.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the probability generating function (PGF) of a branching process? Why is it useful in the analysis of branching processes?**

**Model Answer:**

The PGF of the offspring random variable $\xi$ is
$$G(s) = E[s^{\xi}] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} p_k s^k, \qquad |s|\le 1.$$

It is useful because:
- $R_0 = G'(1)$ (mean offspring in one derivative).
- $\sigma^2 = G''(1) + G'(1) - (G'(1))^2$ (variance).
- $G_{X_{n+1}}(s) = G_{X_n}(G(s))$ converts the *sum of independent copies* into a simple functional composition.
- The fixed-point equation $q = G(q)$ gives the extinction probability in closed form for many offspring laws.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the equation satisfied by the extinction probability $q$ of a Galton–Watson branching process whose offspring distribution has PGF $G(s)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Suppose each individual independently produces either $0$ or $2$ offspring with probabilities $p_0 = 1/4$ and $p_2 = 3/4$. Compute $R_0$, the extinction probability $q$, and the expected population size in generation $n$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Let $q_n = P(X_n = 0)$. By conditioning on $X_n$:
$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{n+1} &= \sum_{k=0}^\infty P(X_n = k)\, P\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^k \xi_i = 0\right) \\
&= \sum_{k=0}^\infty P(X_n = k)\,[G(0)]^k = G_{X_n}\!\bigl(G(0)\bigr).
\end{aligned}
$$

As $n\to\infty$, $q_n \to q$ and under continuity $q = G(q)$. Therefore $q$ is the **smallest non-negative fixed point** of $G$ in $[0,1]$. $\blacksquare$

**(b)** Offspring PGF:
$$
\begin{aligned}
G(s) &= \tfrac14 + \tfrac34 s^2.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mean (Basic Reproduction Number):**
$$R_0 = G'(1) = 2 \cdot \tfrac34 \cdot 1 = \tfrac{3}{2}.$$

**Extinction probability:** Solve $q = \tfrac14 + \tfrac34 q^2$, i.e., $3q^2 - 4q + 1 = 0$:
$$
q = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 12}}{6} = \frac{4 \pm 2}{6} \in \left\{1, \tfrac{1}{3}\right\}.
$$

The **smallest non-negative root** is $q = 1/3$ (since $R_0 > 1$, the survival prob $1 - q = 2/3 > 0$).

**Expected size of generation $n$:**
$$E[X_n] = R_0^{\,n} = \left(\tfrac{3}{2}\right)^{\!n}.$$

For example, $E[X_{10}] = (3/2)^{10} \approx 57.665$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Show that the early-phase SIR epidemic on a configuration-model network can be approximated by a branching process, and derive the epidemic threshold $T_c$ in terms of degree moments. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an ER random graph $G(N,p)$ with mean degree $c = 5$, calculate the transmissibility $T_c$ below which an outbreak cannot occur. Also comment on what happens as $c \to \infty$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** In the early phase of SIR, the density of susceptibles is approximately $1$, so each infectious individual independently infects each susceptible neighbour with probability $T = \beta/\gamma$ before recovering. Pick a random edge and follow it to a node of degree $k$: the number of *other* edges this node has is $k-1$ (excess degree), and each transmits independently with probability $T$. Hence the expected number of offspring per infected edge is
$$R_0 = T \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 - k\rangle}{\langle k\rangle}.$$

When $R_0 \le 1$, the branching process is sub-/critical and dies out with probability 1. Setting $R_0 = 1$:
$$T_c = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 - k \rangle}. \quad \blacksquare$$

**(b)** For $G(N,p)$, the degree distribution is Poisson, so
$$\langle k\rangle = c, \qquad \langle k^2\rangle = c + c^2.$$

Substituting:
$$T_c = \frac{c}{c + c^2 - c} = \frac{c}{c^2} = \frac{1}{c}.$$

For $c = 5$: $\boxed{T_c = 1/5 = 0.2}$. Below this, no epidemic.

As $c \to \infty$, $T_c \to 0$: in dense networks an arbitrarily small per-contact transmissibility suffices to start a giant outbreak (homogeneous-mixing limit).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Choosing the wrong root** of $q = G(q)$. Always pick the **smallest** non-negative root in $[0,1]$. Writing $q=1$ when $R_0>1$ costs **2–3 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting $R_0 = G'(1)$ is the *derivative at 1*, not the value.** Apply L'Hôpital or differentiate term-by-term.
> 3. **Mis-identifying the offspring variable.** Offspring = new infections along *remaining* edges, **not** total degree. Use **excess degree** $\langle k^2-k\rangle / \langle k\rangle$.
> 4. **Confusing the branching PGF with the degree PGF.** The offspring PGF depends on $T$ and excess degree; the degree PGF is purely topological.
> 5. **Skipping the branch-independence justification** when writing $G_{X_{n+1}} = G_{X_n}(G(s))$ — KTU examiners want *one line* explaining the i.i.d. reproduction.
> 6. **Using $E[X_n] = nR_0$** (linear) instead of $E[X_n] = R_0^n$ (exponential). A classic blunder.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **branching process** evolves one generation at a time: $X_{n+1}$ = sum of i.i.d. offspring of $X_n$ parents.
- The **PGF** $G(s) = \sum_k p_k s^k$ encodes the entire offspring law; **$R_0 = G'(1)$** is the mean offspring.
- **Extinction** is the smallest fixed point of $q = G(q)$ in $[0,1]$; $q=1$ when $R_0 \le 1$.
- $E[X_n] = R_0^{\,n}$ — **exponential** growth/decay.
- **Threshold rule:** $R_0 < 1$ ⇒ dies out for sure; $R_0 > 1$ ⇒ survives with positive probability $1-q$.
- On networks, branching approximation is valid in the **early phase** of SIR when susceptibles are plentiful.
- **Epidemic threshold:** $T_c = \langle k\rangle / \langle k^2 - k\rangle$; for ER graphs, $T_c = 1/c$.
- **Criticality = structural change:** at $T_c$ the giant epidemic component appears (percolation transition).
- For non-Poisson offspring (geometric, etc.), solve $q = G(q)$ algebraically or numerically.
- **Multi-type** branching processes (one PGF per "type" of node) handle heterogeneous networks.
- Always specify whether you mean **per-edge** transmissibility $T$ or **per-contact** probability $p$ — they differ by a factor of mean excess degree.
- Final-size equation: $S = 1 - G_1(1 - T + T U)$ where $U$ is the probability an edge leads to a finite subtree — useful beyond early-phase branching.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building Robustness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Building Robustness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In Network Science, **Robustness** is formally defined as the ability of a complex network to maintain its fundamental structural and functional integrity (i.e., its connectivity and operational capacity) when subjected to component failures, environmental perturbations, or deliberate adversarial attacks. Mathematically, it quantifies the fraction of nodes or edges that must be removed before the network disintegrates into disconnected components.

The dual property, **Fragility**, describes the susceptibility of a network to rapid collapse under stress. The KTU 2024 framework distinguishes robustness along two primary axes:
- **Error Tolerance (R)**: Resilience against random failures (e.g., hardware malfunction).
- **Attack Tolerance**: Resilience against targeted removal of high-centrality nodes (e.g., hub nodes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The robustness of a network is not a single scalar value; it is a *systemic property* emerging from the interplay between degree distribution, clustering coefficient, and community structure. For KTU 2024, focus on comparing **Scale-Free** vs **Random** networks under failure.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a city's road network during a natural disaster:
- **Random Failure Analogy**: An earthquake randomly cracks 30% of roads. If the city is a *scale-free network* (with highways acting as hubs), traffic still flows because most commuters rely on the intact highways.
- **Targeted Attack Analogy**: Terrorists specifically bomb the 5 most critical highway interchanges (hubs). Suddenly, the entire city gridlocks and fragments into isolated neighborhoods, even though only 1% of roads were destroyed.

This illustrates the central paradox of network science: **Scale-free networks are remarkably robust to random errors but extremely fragile to targeted attacks.** The internet, power grids, and biological networks all exhibit this property.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition**: Network Robustness is typically quantified by the **Robustness Index** $R$, which measures the area under the curve of the largest connected component size $S(q)$ as a function of node removal fraction $q$. The larger $R$, the more robust the network.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Summary

| Metric | Symbol | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Critical Fraction** | $f_c$ | Minimum fraction of nodes to remove to collapse the giant component |
| **Robustness Index** | $R$ | Normalized area under $S(q)$ curve ($0 \leq R \leq 0.5$) |
| **Average Path Length** | $\langle L \rangle$ | Increases as nodes fail (indicator of degradation) |
| **Percolation Threshold** | $p_c$ | Critical probability below which connectivity breaks |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Network Robustness

Network robustness is governed by **Percolation Theory**, the mathematical framework describing connectivity transitions in random graphs. The analytical breakdown follows:

**Step 1 — Establish the Network Ensemble:**
Determine whether the network is Erdős–Rényi (ER) random, Scale-Free (SF), or a small-world model. Each has a distinct percolation signature.

**Step 2 — Define the Failure Model:**
- *Random Failure (Error)*: Each node $i$ is removed with uniform probability $q$ (so retained with $p = 1-q$).
- *Targeted Attack*: Nodes are removed in descending order of a centrality metric (degree, betweenness, PageRank).

**Step 3 — Compute the Giant Component Fraction $S(q)$:**
The fraction of nodes belonging to the largest connected cluster after removing a fraction $q$ of vertices. The network is considered "functional" as long as $S(q) > 0$.

**Step 4 — Identify the Critical Threshold $f_c$:**
The minimum $q$ at which $S(q) \to 0$, marking the percolation phase transition.

**Step 5 — Evaluate Robustness Index $R$:**
A scalar summary obtained by integrating $S(q)$ over the full range of removals.

**The Why:** This procedure allows engineers to predict the *breaking point* of infrastructure networks (power grids, communication systems) before they actually fail, enabling proactive hardening.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formulas for KTU 2024 Board Exams — Memorize These!**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_c = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}$ | Molloy–Reed criterion for percolation threshold | Determining if giant component exists |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{q=1/N}^{1} S(q)$ | Schneider's Robustness Index | Comparing networks under attack |
| 3 | $f_c^{ER} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$ | Critical removal fraction for ER networks | Random failure analysis |
| 4 | $S(p) = p \, [1 - u(p)]$ | Giant component fraction with generating function | Analytical robustness calculation |
| 5 | $u = 1 - p + p \, G_1(u)$ | Self-consistency equation (generating function) | Solves $S(p)$ for any degree distribution |
| 6 | $\kappa = \dfrac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}$ | Heterogeneity ratio (epidemic/spreading threshold) | Predicts cascading vulnerability |
| 7 | $q_c^{hub} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa - 1}}$ | Targeted attack threshold (approx.) | Hub removal scenarios |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Network | Robustness Concern |
|---|---|---|
| Power Systems | Smart Grids | Cascading blackouts |
| Internet | Autonomous Systems (AS) | Routing resilience under DDoS |
| Biology | Protein-Protein Interaction | Essential gene removal |
| Transportation | Airline / Road Networks | Hub airport closure |
| Social Networks | Online Platforms | Misinformation containment |

The principle of **building robustness** directly informs infrastructure design: engineers add redundant paths (edges), decentralize critical hubs, and introduce *adaptive rerouting* protocols to ensure that the network remains functional under partial failure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Percolation Threshold for ER Networks (Molloy–Reed Criterion)

**Goal:** Derive the critical node occupation probability $p_c$ for an arbitrary degree distribution $P(k)$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Molloy–Reed Condition:**
A giant connected component exists in a network if and only if:

$$\langle k^2 \rangle - 2\langle k \rangle > 0$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Survival Probability $u$:**
Let $u$ be the probability that following a randomly chosen edge, we do *not* reach the giant component via the node at its other end. For a network with occupation probability $p$:

$$u = 1 - p + p \cdot \sum_{k} \dfrac{k P(k)}{\langle k \rangle} u^{k-1}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the Generating Function:**
Define $G_1(x) = \sum_k \dfrac{k P(k)}{\langle k \rangle} x^{k-1}$ (excess degree distribution). Then:

$$u = 1 - p + p \cdot G_1(u)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Giant Component Fraction:**
The probability that a node belongs to the giant component is:

$$S(p) = p \, [1 - G_0(u)]$$

where $G_0(x) = \sum_k P(k) x^k$.

**Step 5 — Find the Critical Threshold $p_c$:**
A non-trivial solution for $u$ appears when the derivative of the right-hand side at $u = 1$ equals 1:

$$\dfrac{d}{du}[1 - p + p \, G_1(u)] \Big\vert_{u=1} = p \cdot G_1'(1) = 1$$

Since $G_1'(1) = \dfrac{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}$, we obtain:

$$p_c = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}$$

**Step 6 — Specialize to ER Networks:**
For ER graphs, $P(k) = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle^k e^{-\langle k \rangle}}{k!}$ (Poisson). Then $\langle k^2 \rangle = \langle k \rangle^2 + \langle k \rangle$. Substituting:

$$p_c^{ER} = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle^2} = \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$$

Equivalently, the critical removal fraction is:

$$f_c^{ER} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$$

**Interpretation:** For an ER network with mean degree $\langle k \rangle = 5$, we must remove **80%** of nodes before the network disintegrates — extremely robust!

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Robustness Index $R$ for Targeted Attack on Scale-Free Network

**Setup:** Scale-free network with degree distribution $P(k) = c \, k^{-\gamma}$, where $2 < \gamma < 3$ (typical for real networks).

**Step 1 — Compute Heterogeneity Ratio $\kappa$:**
For $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$:

$$\langle k \rangle = \int_{m}^{K} k \cdot c \, k^{-\gamma} dk$$

$$\langle k^2 \rangle = \int_{m}^{K} k^2 \cdot c \, k^{-\gamma} dk$$

Assuming large $K$ and $2 < \gamma < 3$, the second moment **diverges** as $K \to \infty$, making $\kappa \to \infty$.

**Step 2 — Apply Targeted Attack Threshold:**

$$q_c \approx 1 - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\kappa - 1}}$$

Since $\kappa$ is very large:

$$q_c \to 1 \quad \text{(but finite threshold exists for finite networks)}$$

For finite scale-free networks with $N = 10^4$ nodes and $\gamma = 2.5$:

$$q_c \approx 0.05 \text{ to } 0.10$$

**Step 3 — Compute $R$ via Discrete Sum:**
For a network of $N$ nodes, removing them one by one (in decreasing order of degree):

$$R = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{Q=1}^{N} \dfrac{S(Q)}{N}$$

where $S(Q)$ is the size of the largest connected component after $Q$ removals.

**Step 4 — Comparative Result:**
Typical values reported in Network Science literature:

| Network Type | $R$ (Random Failure) | $R$ (Targeted Attack) |
|---|---|---|
| **Erdős–Rényi** | $\approx 0.40$ | $\approx 0.20$ |
| **Scale-Free ($\gamma = 2.5$)** | $\approx 0.48$ | $\approx 0.08$ |
| **Random Regular** | $\approx 0.35$ | $\approx 0.35$ |

This table encapsulates the fundamental paradox: **Scale-Free networks are the most robust to random failure but the most fragile to targeted attack.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cascading Failure Threshold (Motter–Lai Model)

The **Motter–Lai model** captures cascading overload collapse. Each node $i$ has capacity $C_i = (1 + \alpha) L_i$, where $L_i$ is the initial betweenness load and $\alpha \geq 0$ is the tolerance parameter.

When a node is removed, load redistributes. Failure occurs if any node receives load $> C_i$.

**Critical Tolerance Threshold:**

$$\alpha_c = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle - 2}{\langle k \rangle - 1} \cdot \dfrac{N}{\langle k^2 \rangle - 2\langle k \rangle}$$

For ER networks with $\langle k \rangle = 4$: $\alpha_c \approx 0.40$ — meaning a 40% capacity buffer is needed to prevent cascading collapse.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Failure Mode Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Network Robustness Analysis] --> B{Failure Type?}
    B -->|Random Failure| C[Error Tolerance Model]
    B -->|Targeted Attack| D[Attack Tolerance Model]

    C --> C1[Select Node Uniformly at Random]
    C1 --> C2[Remove with Probability q]
    C2 --> C3[Compute S of q]
    C3 --> C4{S greater than 0?}
    C4 -->|Yes| C5[Network Survives]
    C4 -->|No| C6[Network Collapses]

    D --> D1[Rank Nodes by Centrality]
    D1 --> D2[Remove Highest-Centrality First]
    D2 --> D3[Compute S of q]
    D3 --> D4{S greater than 0?}
    D4 -->|Yes| D5[Network Survives]
    D4 -->|No| D6[Network Collapses]

    C6 --> E[Robustness Index R computed]
    D6 --> E
    C5 --> E
    D5 --> E
    E --> F[Compare R values across topologies]
```

### 4.2 Cascading Failure Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    INIT[Initial Network State] --> LOAD[Compute Node Loads L_i]
    LOAD --> CAP[Assign Capacities C_i equals 1 plus alpha times L_i]
    CAP --> TRIG[Trigger Event: Remove Node X]
    TRIG --> REDIST[Load Redistribution along Shortest Paths]
    REDIST --> CHECK{Any Node Load exceeds C_i?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| FAIL[Mark Node as Failed]
    CHECK -->|No| STABLE[System Stable]
    FAIL --> REDIST
    STABLE --> OUT[End Simulation: Fraction Survived equals S]
```

### 4.3 Percolation Phase Transition Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Degree Distribution P of k] --> P2[Compute Mean Degree and Second Moment]
    P2 --> P3[Apply Molloy-Reed Criterion]
    P3 --> P4{Second Moment minus 2 Mean greater than 0?}
    P4 -->|Yes| P5[Giant Component Exists]
    P4 -->|No| P6[Only Finite Clusters]
    P5 --> P7[Find Critical Fraction f_c]
    P7 --> P8[Network is Robust if f_c is high]
    P8 --> P9[Design Strategies: Add Redundant Edges]
    P6 --> P10[Network is Fragile: Rebuild Required]
```

### 4.4 Robustness Strategy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Goal: Build Robust Network] --> S2{Strategy Selection}
    S2 --> S3[Edge Redundancy: Add Backup Links]
    S2 --> S4[Degree Distribution: Avoid Heavy Tails]
    S2 --> S5[Community Overlap: Bridge Modules]
    S2 --> S6[Capacity Tuning: Set alpha greater than alpha_c]
    S3 --> S7[Higher R index achieved]
    S4 --> S7
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    S7 --> S8[Resilient Infrastructure Deployed]
```
