The hub has $R_0$ that is $N-1$ times larger than a leaf.

**Step 4 — Conclusion [1 mark]:**

The **hub is by far the most effective patient zero**. Infecting the hub produces $\sim (N-1)$ secondary infections on average, while a leaf can only infect the hub (1 secondary infection). Targeting hubs for vaccination/quarantine is the most efficient containment strategy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1 (–2 marks):** Writing $\rho^* = \frac{1}{R_0}$ instead of $\rho^* = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$. The correct formula is **minus** $1/R_0$, not reciprocal.
> - **Common Mistake 2 (–2 marks):** Using the HMF threshold on heterogeneous networks. For scale-free or heterogeneous networks, you **must** use $\frac{\beta \langle k^2 \rangle}{\mu \langle k \rangle} > 1$.
> - **Common Mistake 3 (–1 mark):** Forgetting the conservation constraint $s + \rho = 1$, leading to an unsolvable two-variable system.
> - **Common Mistake 4 (–2 marks):** Confusing SIS with SIR. In SIS, recovered nodes **re-enter the susceptible class** — there is no permanent immunity.
> - **Always** state the **epidemic threshold condition** explicitly when deriving equilibria. Examiners deduct marks for omitting the condition $R_0 > 1$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SIS Model**

- **Two states only:** Susceptible (S) and Infected (I). No recovered class with immunity.
- **Key parameters:** $\beta$ (infection rate), $\mu$ (recovery rate), $\tau = 1/\mu$ (infectious period).
- **HMF equation:** $\frac{d\rho}{dt} = \rho \left[ \beta \langle k \rangle (1 - \rho) - \mu \right]$.
- **Endemic steady state:** $\rho^* = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$ where $R_0 = \frac{\beta \langle k \rangle}{\mu}$.
- **Epidemic threshold:** $R_0 = 1$. Above it, disease persists; below it, disease dies out.
- **Heterogeneous threshold:** $R_0^{\text{het}} = \frac{\beta \langle k^2 \rangle}{\mu \langle k \rangle}$.
- **Scale-free networks with $\gamma \leq 3$:** Threshold vanishes; endemic state exists for any $\beta > 0$.
- **NIMFA:** Node-level approximation using adjacency matrix $A_{ij}$; more accurate than HMF.
- **Conservation law:** $s(t) + \rho(t) = 1$ at all times.
- **Stability criterion:** $f'(0) = \beta \langle k \rangle - \mu$; sign determines DFE stability.
- **Difference from SIR:** SIS has $S \to I \to S$ cycle (no immunity); SIR has $S \to I \to R$ (permanent immunity).
- **Real-world applications:** Endemic diseases, computer malware, rumor/meme propagation, viral marketing.
- **Most influential node:** High-degree hubs are the most effective spreaders and the most strategic targets for containment.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) Model # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) Model** is a foundational **compartmental epidemiological model** in network science used to mathematically describe the temporal progression of an infectious disease through a closed, homogeneously-mixed population. The model partitions the total population $N$ into three mutually exclusive epidemiological compartments: 1. **Susceptible ($S$)** — Individuals who have not yet contracted the disease but are vulnerable to infection. 2. **Infected ($I$)** — Individuals who are currently infected and capable of transmitting the pathogen to susceptible individuals. 3. **Recovered ($R$)** — Individuals who have either recovered from the disease (and acquired immunity) or have been removed from the transmission chain (through isolation, recovery, or death). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The SIR model is treated as the canonical starting point for studying **disease dynamics on networks**. In a network-theoretic setting, the classical mean-field SIR equations are modified to account for the underlying contact graph topology, where the term $\beta S I$ in mean-field theory is replaced by $\beta S \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} I_j / k_i$ in network-based formulations. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation Imagine a **closed classroom of 100 students** where one student walks in with the flu. - Every student who has **NOT** yet had the flu is labeled **Susceptible** — they are sitting ducks, waiting to be exposed. - The sick student is **Infected** — every time they sneeze near a susceptible classmate, there is a probability of transmission. - Students who recover from the flu are placed in the **Recovered** compartment — they cannot catch the same flu again (immunity). The disease progresses as a **flow** of individuals: $S \rightarrow I \rightarrow R$. Just like water flowing through a pipe network, susceptible people "leak" into the infected pool, and infected people eventually "drain" into the recovered pool. The model tracks how fast this flow happens using two key constants: the **transmission rate** ($\beta$) and the **recovery rate** ($\gamma$). > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The SIR model assumes that recovered individuals gain **permanent immunity**. This is why it is particularly suited to diseases like measles, chickenpox, and influenza (in idealized form), but NOT to the common cold or sexually transmitted diseases where immunity wanes or reinfection is possible. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Parameters The following are the **standard parameters** universally used in KTU-board questions on the SIR model: - **$\beta$ (Beta)** — **Transmission/Infection rate** (per unit time). Represents the probability per contact that a susceptible becomes infected, multiplied by the contact frequency. - **$\gamma$ (Gamma)** — **Recovery rate** (per unit time). The reciprocal $\gamma^{-1}$ gives the average duration of infection. - **$R_0$** — **Basic Reproduction Number**: the expected number of secondary infections caused by a single infected individual in a fully susceptible population. - **$N$** — **Total population** (assumed constant: $S + I + R = N$). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — How the SIR Model Works The SIR model functions through a **system of coupled, nonlinear ordinary differential equations (ODEs)**. The mechanics can be broken down into the following logical steps: ### Step 1: Compartmentalization The entire population $N$ is divided into three disjoint sets. There is **no overlap** — an individual belongs to exactly one compartment at any given moment $t$. ### Step 2: Assumptions of the Classical (Mean-Field) SIR Model - The population is **closed** (no births, deaths by other causes, immigration, or emigration). - Mixing is **homogeneous** (every individual has equal probability of contacting every other). - The incubation period is **negligible** (an infected individual becomes infectious immediately). - Recovery confers **lifelong immunity**. - $N = S(t) + I(t) + R(t)$ is conserved over time. ### Step 3: Transition Rates Between Compartments - A susceptible individual becomes infected at a rate proportional to both their fraction and the infected fraction: $\beta \cdot \frac{S}{N} \cdot I$. - An infected individual recovers at rate $\gamma \cdot I$ (independent of $S$ or $R$). ### Step 4: Governing Differential Equations The model's evolution is fully described by: $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{dS}{dt} &= -\beta \frac{S \cdot I}{N} \\ \frac{dI}{dt} &= +\beta \frac{S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I \\ \frac{dR}{dt} &= +\gamma \cdot I \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Simplification (per capita form):** Many textbooks drop the $1/N$ normalization and write $\beta S I$, implicitly assuming $N = 1$ (fractional population). For KTU board answers, **always specify the convention you are using** — this is a frequent source of lost marks. ### Step 5: The Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ From the infected equation, we can factor $I$: $$ \frac{dI}{dt} = I \left( \beta \frac{S}{N} - \gamma \right) $$ At the start of an outbreak, $S \approx N$, so the early-growth rate is governed by $(\beta - \gamma)$. The **basic reproduction number** is therefore: $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} $$ - If $R_0 > 1$: the disease **spreads** (epidemic). - If $R_0 < 1$: the disease **dies out**. - If $R_0 = 1$: the disease is in **endemic equilibrium**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Unit** | |:-------------|:------------|:------------|:---------| | $\frac{dS}{dt}$ | $-\beta \frac{S I}{N}$ | Rate of loss from Susceptible | persons / time | | $\frac{dI}{dt}$ | $\beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I$ | Rate of change of Infected | persons / time | | $\frac{dR}{dt}$ | $\gamma I$ | Rate of gain to Recovered | persons / time | | $R_0$ (Basic Reproduction Number) | $\frac{\beta}{\gamma}$ | Avg. secondary infections per case | dimensionless | | $R(t)$ (Effective Reproduction Number) | $\frac{\beta}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{S(t)}{N}$ | Time-varying reproduction number | dimensionless | | Herd Immunity Threshold | $1 - \frac{1}{R_0}$ | Fraction to vaccinate to stop outbreak | dimensionless | | Mean Infectious Period | $\frac{1}{\gamma}$ | Average time spent in $I$ compartment | time | | Peak Infection Time | Solution of $\frac{S}{N} = \frac{1}{R_0}$ | Time of maximum $I(t)$ | time | | Conservation Law | $S + I + R = N$ | Total population invariant | persons | | Phase Plane Invariant | $\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\gamma}{\beta S} - 1$ | Trajectory in $S$-$I$ plane | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** When using $\vert S \vert$, $\vert I \vert$, or $\vert N \vert$ in your answer sheet, always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ for absolute value. **Never use the bare vertical bar** inside a table cell, as it breaks the markdown table syntax in some renderers. ## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Public Health The SIR model is the **workhorse of mathematical epidemiology** and is used for: - **Outbreak forecasting**: Predicting peak hospital demand (COVID-19, influenza, Ebola). - **Vaccination policy**: Determining the herd-immunity threshold for a disease. - **Network intervention strategies**: Identifying "super-spreaders" by replacing mean-field $\beta S I$ with $\beta S \sum_{j} a_{ij} I_j$, where $a_{ij}$ is the adjacency matrix of the contact network. - **Cybersecurity**: Modeling malware propagation across computer networks, where "infected" machines infect "susceptible" ones, and "recovered" machines are patched and immunized. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Governing ODEs from First Principles **Goal:** Derive the three SIR differential equations from the assumption of mass-action incidence. **Step 1.1:** Define the susceptible population $S(t)$. A susceptible individual enters the infected compartment when they contact an infected individual. The **incidence rate** (new infections per unit time) under the mass-action assumption is: $$ \text{Incidence} = \beta \cdot \frac{S(t)}{N} \cdot I(t) $$ Here, $\beta$ is the per-contact transmission rate, $\frac{S}{N}$ is the probability a randomly contacted person is susceptible, and $I$ is the number of infectious contacts available. The loss rate from $S$ therefore equals this incidence: $$ \boxed{\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N}} $$ **Step 1.2:** Define the infected population $I(t)$. It grows by the same incidence term and shrinks by recovery. Recovery happens at rate $\gamma$ per infected individual: $$ \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot \frac{S \cdot I}{N} - \gamma \cdot I $$ We can factor $I$ for analytical convenience: $$ \boxed{\frac{dI}{dt} = I \left( \beta \cdot \frac{S}{N} - \gamma \right)} $$ **Step 1.3:** Define the recovered population $R(t)$. It grows solely by recovery from $I$: $$ \boxed{\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma \cdot I} $$ **Step 1.4 (Verification of Conservation Law):** Sum all three equations: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} + \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{dR}{dt} = -\beta \frac{SI}{N} + \beta \frac{SI}{N} - \gamma I + \gamma I = 0 $$ Therefore, $S(t) + I(t) + R(t) = S(0) + I(0) + R(0) = N$ is conserved. $\quad \blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ **Goal:** Express $R_0$ rigorously from the linearized early-outbreak dynamics. **Step 2.1:** At $t = 0$, the disease is just introduced. We have $S(0) \approx N$ and $I(0) = I_0 \ll N$. Substituting $S \approx N$ into the $I$ equation: $$ \frac{dI}{dt} \approx I \left( \beta \cdot \frac{N}{N} - \gamma \right) = I(\beta - \gamma) $$ **Step 2.2:** Solve the linearized ODE. This is separable: $$ \frac{dI}{I} = (\beta - \gamma) \, dt $$ Integrating from $0$ to $t$: $$ \ln I(t) - \ln I_0 = (\beta - \gamma) t $$ So the early exponential growth is: $$ \boxed{I(t) \approx I_0 \, e^{(\beta - \gamma)t}} $$ **Step 2.3:** Now, $\beta^{-1}$ is the average time between successive transmissions from one infected person (in a fully susceptible population), and $\gamma^{-1}$ is the average time spent infected. The expected number of secondary infections per primary case is therefore: $$ R_0 = \frac{\text{Transmission rate}}{\text{Recovery rate}} = \boxed{\frac{\beta}{\gamma}} $$ **Step 2.4 (Epidemic Threshold):** For an outbreak to occur, we need $\frac{dI}{dt} > 0$ initially: $$ I_0 (\beta - \gamma) > 0 \iff \beta > \gamma \iff \boxed{R_0 > 1} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Herd Immunity Threshold **Goal:** Find the fraction $f$ of the population that must be immune (recovered/vaccinated) to halt the outbreak. **Step 3.1:** Suppose a fraction $f$ of the population is immune. Then the effective susceptible fraction is $(1 - f)$. **Step 3.2:** The effective reproduction number in this partially immune population is: $$ R_{\text{eff}} = R_0 \cdot (1 - f) $$ **Step 3.3:** For the outbreak to die out, we require $R_{\text{eff}} \leq 1$: $$ R_0 (1 - f) \leq 1 $$ $$ 1 - f \leq \frac{1}{R_0} $$ $$ \boxed{f \geq 1 - \frac{1}{R_0}} $$ **Numerical Example:** For measles ($R_0 \approx 12$ to $18$), the herd immunity threshold is $f \geq 1 - \frac{1}{12} \approx 0.917$, meaning **$\approx 92\%$** of the population must be immune to halt transmission. ## 3.4 Derivation 4: The Phase-Plane Trajectory (S-I Plane) **Goal:** Eliminate time $t$ and obtain the relationship between $S$ and $I$ directly. **Step 4.1:** Divide $\frac{dI}{dt}$ by $\frac{dS}{dt}$: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I}{-\beta \frac{S I}{N}} = -1 + \frac{\gamma N}{\beta S} $$ **Step 4.2:** Rearrange into the standard phase-plane form: $$ \boxed{\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\gamma N}{\beta S} - 1 = \frac{1}{R_0 \cdot (S/N)} - 1} $$ **Step 4.3 (Interpretation):** The trajectory in the $S$-$I$ plane starts at $(S(0), I(0))$ and moves leftward (as $S$ decreases) while first rising (as $I$ initially grows) and then falling. The **peak infection** occurs where $\frac{dI}{dt} = 0$, which from the factored form requires: $$ \beta \frac{S^*}{N} - \gamma = 0 \implies S^* = \frac{N}{R_0} $$ This is the **critical susceptible threshold**: once $S$ drops below $N/R_0$, the infected curve must decline. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Solutions and Equilibria | **Regime** | **Condition** | **Behavior of $I(t)$** | **Final State** | |:-----------|:--------------|:-----------------------|:----------------| | Subcritical | $R_0 < 1$ | Monotonic decay to 0 | Disease-Free Equilibrium (DFE) | | Critical | $R_0 = 1$ | Constant (trivial equilibrium) | Marginal DFE | | Supercritical | $R_0 > 1$ | Exponential rise, peak, then decay | Endemic Equilibrium (some infected/recovered) | | Final Size | All $t \to \infty$ | $I \to 0$, $R \to R_\infty$ | Determined by transcendental equation $R_\infty = N - S_\infty$, where $S_\infty e^{-R_0 R_\infty / N} = S_0$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Compartmental Flow Diagram (Mermaid) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the classical SIR flow architecture and the role of each parameter. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Population["Closed Population N"] direction LR S(("S Susceptible")) I(("I Infected")) R(("R Recovered")) end S -->|beta * S*I slash N
New Infections| I I -->|gamma * I
Recovery| R I -.->|Mortality or Waning Immunity
Extensions only| Out[("Removed from System")] S -.->|Vac| V[("Vaccinated")] V -.-> R ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows denote **active transitions** in the classical model. Dashed arrows indicate **extensions** (vaccination, mortality) that are not present in the basic SIR formulation but appear in variants like SIRS or SIRV. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Network-Based SIR) When SIR is implemented on a network, the mean-field transmission term $\beta S I$ is replaced by a degree-class-summed term. The block diagram below shows the functional architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph InputLayer["Input Layer"] Net["Contact Network G(V,E)"] Adj["Adjacency Matrix A_ij"] end subgraph ProcessLayer["Processing Layer"] DegCalc["Compute Degree k_i"] NeighborSum["Sum I_j over neighbors j of i"] InfectProb["Compute lambda_i = beta * k_i_inv * Sum"] end subgraph StateLayer["State Update Layer"] Snode["Update S_i"] Inode["Update I_i"] Rnode["Update R_i"] end subgraph OutputLayer["Output"] Plot["Plot I_i t vs Time"] Peak["Detect Peak Infection"] end Net --> Adj Adj --> NeighborSum Net --> DegCalc DegCalc --> NeighborSum NeighborSum --> InfectProb InfectProb --> Snode InfectProb --> Inode Adj --> Inode Inode --> Rnode Snode --> Plot Inode --> Plot Rnode --> Plot Inode --> Peak ``` ## 4.3 Phase-Plane Schematic (Conceptual) A textual schematic of the $S$–$I$ phase plane (drawn from left to right): ``` I(t) ^ | * (I_max at S* = N/R_0) | * | * | * | * | * | * | * |*___________________________> S(t) S_inf S* N (decreasing S) ``` - The trajectory moves **leftward** (S decreases monotonically). - It first **rises** in I until reaching the turning point at $S^* = N/R_0$. - It then **falls** asymptotically toward the $S$-axis (where $I \to 0$). - The total epidemic "burns out" when $S$ reaches the level $S_{\infty}$ where the trajectory crosses the $S$-axis. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define the Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ in the SIR model and state the condition for an epidemic to occur.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$ is defined as the expected number of secondary infections produced by a single infected individual introduced into a **completely susceptible** population. Mathematically, it is the ratio of the transmission rate to the recovery rate: $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} $$ where $\beta$ is the transmission rate and $\gamma$ is the recovery rate. An epidemic occurs if and only if $R_0 > 1$. If $R_0 < 1$, the disease dies out without causing a major outbreak; if $R_0 = 1$, the disease is in a critical state with neither growth nor decline. --- ### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between $R_0$ and the Effective Reproduction Number $R(t)$ in the SIR model.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** $R_0$ is a **constant** that depends only on the intrinsic disease parameters and represents the average number of secondary infections per case in a fully susceptible population: $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} $$ In contrast, the Effective Reproduction Number $R(t)$ is **time-varying** and accounts for the depletion of susceptibles over the course of the epidemic. It is given by: $$ R(t) = R_0 \cdot \frac{S(t)}{N} $$ As $S(t)$ decreases due to infection and recovery, $R(t)$ declines. An outbreak naturally ends when $R(t)$ falls below 1, even if $R_0$ was originally greater than 1. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Derive the governing differential equations of the classical Susceptible-Infected-Recovered (SIR) model from first principles. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A flu outbreak in a closed college campus of $N = 1000$ students has the following parameters: $\beta = 0.5$ per day, $\gamma = 0.25$ per day, $I(0) = 1$, $S(0) = 999$. Compute the Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$, the herd immunity threshold, and determine whether the outbreak will grow. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-A (a): **Assumptions of the classical SIR model:** 1. Closed population (no births, deaths, immigration). 2. Homogeneous mixing. 3. Instantaneous transition from exposure to infectivity. 4. Lifelong immunity after recovery. 5. Incubation period is negligible. **Derivation:** Consider the rate of new infections under mass-action kinetics. A susceptible individual meets an infected individual at a rate proportional to $I/N$. The number of effective transmissions per unit time is $\beta \cdot S \cdot (I/N)$. This is the rate at which $S$ decreases: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \frac{S I}{N} \quad \text{...(i)} $$ The infected compartment grows by the same term and decreases by recovery at rate $\gamma I$: $$ \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I \quad \text{...(ii)} $$ The recovered compartment grows solely by recovery: $$ \frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I \quad \text{...(iii)} $$ **Verification:** Adding (i), (ii), (iii) gives $\frac{d}{dt}(S+I+R) = 0$, confirming conservation of $N$. The system is closed and fully describes the dynamics. **(7 marks distributed across the three equations, the assumptions, and the verification step.)** --- #### Model Solution to B-A (b): **Step 1: Compute $R_0$** $$ R_0 = \frac{\beta}{\gamma} = \frac{0.5}{0.25} = 2 $$ **Step 2: Herd Immunity Threshold** $$ f = 1 - \frac{1}{R_0} = 1 - \frac{1}{2} = 0.5 $$ Thus, at least **50%** of the population must be immune (vaccinated/recovered) to halt the outbreak. **Step 3: Initial Behavior** At $t = 0$, $\frac{dI}{dt} = I_0 (\beta - \gamma) = 1 \cdot (0.5 - 0.25) = 0.25 > 0$. Since $R_0 = 2 > 1$, the outbreak **will grow exponentially** in the early phase. **Step 4: Critical Susceptible Threshold** $$ S^* = \frac{N}{R_0} = \frac{1000}{2} = 500 $$ The infected population peaks when $S(t)$ drops to 500. Beyond this point, $\frac{dI}{dt} < 0$ and the curve declines. **Conclusion:** Since $R_0 = 2 > 1$, the outbreak is in the supercritical regime and will produce an epidemic. Approximately 50% of the campus must be immunized to prevent this. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)* **(a)** Explain the concept of the herd immunity threshold in the SIR model. Derive the formula $f = 1 - 1/R_0$ and explain its public health significance with one numerical example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the phase-plane trajectory equation $\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{1}{R_0 (S/N)} - 1$ for the SIR model. Use it to show that the peak of the epidemic occurs when $S = N/R_0$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-B (a): **Definition:** The herd immunity threshold is the minimum fraction of a population that must be immune to an infectious disease (through vaccination or prior infection) so that the disease cannot sustain transmission and dies out. **Derivation:** Suppose a fraction $f$ of the population is immune. The remaining susceptible fraction is $(1-f)$. The effective reproduction number becomes: $$ R_{\text{eff}} = R_0 \cdot (1-f) $$ For the outbreak to die out, we require $R_{\text{eff}} \leq 1$: $$ R_0 (1-f) \leq 1 \implies 1 - f \leq \frac{1}{R_0} \implies f \geq 1 - \frac{1}{R_0} $$ **Numerical Example (COVID-19, Delta variant):** Assuming $R_0 = 5$: $$ f = 1 - \frac{1}{5} = 0.80 = 80\% $$ Approximately 80% of the population must be vaccinated/immune to halt community transmission. **Public Health Significance:** This threshold guides vaccination campaigns and policy decisions, e.g., the WHO's measles elimination target of 95% coverage corresponds to $R_0 \approx 20$. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to B-B (b): **Step 1:** From the SIR equations: $$ \frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \frac{S I}{N}, \quad \frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I $$ **Step 2:** Apply the chain rule $\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{dI/dt}{dS/dt}$: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{\beta \frac{S I}{N} - \gamma I}{-\beta \frac{S I}{N}} $$ **Step 3:** Factor $I$ from the numerator and divide: $$ \frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{I \left( \beta \frac{S}{N} - \gamma \right)}{-\beta \frac{S I}{N}} = \frac{\beta \frac{S}{N} - \gamma}{-\beta \frac{S}{N}} = -1 + \frac{\gamma N}{\beta S} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute $R_0 = \beta/\gamma$ to get the final form: $$ \boxed{\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{1}{R_0 (S/N)} - 1} $$ **Step 5 (Peak Condition):** The peak of $I(t)$ occurs when $\frac{dI}{dt} = 0$. From the infected equation: $$ I \left( \beta \frac{S}{N} - \gamma \right) = 0 $$ Since $I \neq 0$ during the epidemic: $$ \beta \frac{S^*}{N} = \gamma \implies \frac{S^*}{N} = \frac{\gamma}{\beta} = \frac{1}{R_0} $$ $$ \boxed{S^* = \frac{N}{R_0}} $$ This confirms that the peak occurs precisely when the susceptible population drops to the threshold $N/R_0$. **(7 marks)** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in SIR Problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the $1/N$ factor**: Many students write $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta S I$ instead of $-\beta \frac{SI}{N}$. Either include $N$ consistently or explicitly state "with $N=1$ for simplicity." Loss: up to **2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing $R_0$ with $R(t)$**: $R_0$ is a constant; $R(t)$ depends on $S(t)$. Mixing these two loses **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Not verifying the conservation law**: Adding $\frac{dS}{dt} + \frac{dI}{dt} + \frac{dR}{dt} = 0$ is a free mark-grabber — never skip it. > 4. **Skipping units or dimensions**: Always mention that $\beta$ and $\gamma$ have units of [time]$^{-1}$, and $R_0$ is dimensionless. > 5. **Ignoring the assumption of homogeneous mixing**: For network-based SIR extensions, you MUST mention the underlying contact graph topology — partial credit is heavily penalized otherwise. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for the SIR Model:** - ☐ **Three compartments**: $S$, $I$, $R$ — mutually exclusive and exhaustive over $N$. - ☐ **Three ODEs**: $\frac{dS}{dt} = -\beta \frac{SI}{N}$, $\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \frac{SI}{N} - \gamma I$, $\frac{dR}{dt} = \gamma I$. - ☐ **Conservation Law**: $S + I + R = N$ at all times. - ☐ **Basic Reproduction Number**: $R_0 = \beta / \gamma$. Threshold for outbreak: $R_0 > 1$. - ☐ **Effective Reproduction Number**: $R(t) = R_0 \cdot S(t)/N$ — time-varying, decreases as $S$ depletes. - ☐ **Herd Immunity Threshold**: $f = 1 - 1/R_0$ — fraction to vaccinate. - ☐ **Mean Infectious Period**: $1/\gamma$ — average time spent in $I$. - ☐ **Peak Condition**: Infection peaks when $S = N/R_0$. - ☐ **Phase-Plane Invariant**: $\frac{dI}{dS} = \frac{1}{R_0 (S/N)} - 1$. - ☐ **Network Extension**: On a graph, $\beta SI$ becomes $\beta S_i \sum_{j \in \mathcal{N}(i)} I_j / k_i$. - ☐ **Endemic vs Disease-Free Equilibria**: $I^* = 0$ always exists; endemic requires $R_0 > 1$. - ☐ **Assumptions**: Closed population, homogeneous mixing, lifelong immunity, instantaneous infectivity. - ☐ **Variants to be aware of**: SIS (no immunity), SIRS (waning immunity), SEIR (exposed/latent), SIRV (vaccination). - ☐ **Engineering analogies**: Malware spread, rumor propagation, information cascades — all map naturally to SIR.

topics.title: Network Epidemics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Network Epidemics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Network Epidemics** is the branch of Network Science that studies the **propagation of diseases, viruses, ideas, rumors, or failures** across a contact network whose nodes (individuals/computers) and edges (contact/transmission channels) are explicitly represented. Unlike classical **mean-field compartmental epidemiology** which assumes perfectly mixed populations, Network Epidemics models the **heterogeneous topology** of real-world contact structures to predict outbreak size, duration, threshold, and targeted immunization strategies.

The discipline intersects graph theory, statistical physics, and mathematical epidemiology. Formally, an epidemic process is a **continuous-time stochastic dynamical system** defined on a graph $G(V,E)$ where each node transitions between discrete health states.

### The Three Canonical Compartmental Models

| Model | States Allowed | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **SI** | Susceptible $\to$ Infected (irreversible) | Common cold (no recovery) |
| **SIS** | Susceptible $\leftrightarrow$ Infected (cyclic) | Flu, computer viruses |
| **SIR** | Susceptible $\to$ Infected $\to$ Recovered (immune) | Measles, COVID-19 |

> [!NOTE]
> The **transmissibility** $T$ (or infection probability per contact) and the **recovery rate** $\mu$ are the two fundamental control parameters. The ratio $\lambda = T/\mu$ is called the **effective spreading rate**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **college campus outbreak** of the flu. In the classical (well-mixed) view, every student is equally likely to meet every other student — clearly unrealistic. In reality:
- **Hostel A** has a central, highly social student who knows everyone — a **hub node**.
- **Hostel B** has introverted students with only 2 friends each — **low-degree nodes**.
- The flu will explode through Hostel A but smolder in Hostel B.

**Network Epidemics** quantifies this intuition: it tells us *why* targeting one hub for vaccination can stop an outbreak more effectively than vaccinating 100 random low-degree students. This is the cornerstone of **targeted immunization** strategies.

### Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Basic Reproduction Number $R_0$**: Expected number of secondary infections produced by a single infected individual in a fully susceptible population. **Critical threshold: $R_0 = 1$.**
- **Epidemic Threshold $\tau_c$**: Minimum transmissibility required for an outbreak to spread macroscopically.
- **Prevalence $\rho$**: Fraction of infected nodes at a given time.
- **Degree $k$**: Number of connections of a node.
- **Degree Heterogeneity $\langle k^2 \rangle$**: Second moment of the degree distribution — measures how "hub-dominated" the network is.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight**: On heterogeneous networks (like scale-free), the epidemic threshold $\tau_c \to 0$ as the network size $N \to \infty$. This means an arbitrarily small infection rate can cause a **macroscopic outbreak** — a profound result with no classical epidemiological equivalent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanics — Step-by-Step Logic Flow

### Step 1: Define the State Space
Each node $i \in V$ holds a discrete state $x_i(t) \in \{S, I, R\}$ depending on the chosen compartmental model. Transitions occur at rates determined by neighbors' states.

### Step 2: Define the Transition Rules
- **SI / SIS**: At rate $T$, a susceptible node $S$ with at least one infected neighbor becomes infected.
- **SIR**: After recovery, an infected node transitions to $R$ at rate $\mu$ and **never** returns to $S$.
- **SIS**: Recovery sends $I \to S$, allowing reinfection.

### Step 3: Identify the Two Fundamental Quantities
- **Transmissibility $T$**: Probability of infection transmission per edge contact per unit time.
- **Recovery rate $\mu$**: Probability of recovery per unit time.
- **Spreading rate**: $\lambda = T/\mu$.

### Step 4: Determine the Epidemic Threshold
A network supports a **macroscopic outbreak** iff $\lambda > \tau_c$, where $\tau_c$ depends on the network's spectral and degree properties.

### Step 5: Compute Steady-State Prevalence
After long times ($t \to \infty$), the prevalence $\rho_\infty$ characterizes the endemic (SIS) or final (SIR) infection level.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

The **mean-field assumption** simplifies interactions by replacing exact neighbor states with their average densities. This is what makes analytical predictions tractable. The choice of mean-field approximation depends on the network type:

- **Homogeneous Mean-Field (HMF)**: Used for random regular or Erdős–Rényi graphs where $\langle k^2 \rangle \approx \langle k \rangle^2$.
- **Heterogeneous Mean-Field (HMF/HET)**: Used for scale-free or degree-correlated networks where degree classes behave distinctly.
- **Quasi-Chemical Mean-Field**: Tracks $S$–$I$ and $I$–$I$ edge densities — more accurate.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The High-Yield Formula Sheet — Memorize This Table for KTU Exams**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Applicability | Key Variable Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Effective spreading rate | $\lambda = T / \mu$ | All models | $T$: transmission, $\mu$: recovery |
| 2 | Epidemic threshold (SIS, HMF) | $\tau_c = \dfrac{\mu}{T} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle}$ | Random homogeneous | $\langle k \rangle$: mean degree |
| 3 | Epidemic threshold (SIS, HET) | $\tau_c = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle}$ | Heterogeneous | $\langle k^2 \rangle$: 2nd moment |
| 4 | SIR final size (HMF) | $R_\infty = 1 - e^{-\lambda R_\infty \langle k \rangle / \mu}$ | Random graph | Self-consistency |
| 5 | Steady-state prevalence SIS (HMF) | $\rho \approx 1 - \dfrac{\tau_c}{\lambda}$ | Above threshold | Valid for $\lambda \gtrsim \tau_c$ |
| 6 | Steady-state prevalence SIS (HET) | $\rho_k = \dfrac{\lambda k \Theta}{1 + \lambda k \Theta}$ | Degree-class $k$ | $\Theta$: infected neighbor prob. |
| 7 | Targeted immunization threshold | $\tau_c^{(imm)} = \dfrac{\tau_c}{1 - g_c}$ | Targeted strategy | $g_c$: critical immunized fraction |
| 8 | Random immunization threshold | $g_c = 1 - \dfrac{\tau_c}{\lambda}$ | Random strategy | Compared to targeted |
| 9 | Spectral threshold (general) | $\tau_c = \dfrac{1}{\Lambda_{\max}}$ | All networks | $\Lambda_{\max}$: largest eigenvalue of adjacency |
| 10 | Basic reproduction $R_0$ (networks) | $R_0 = \lambda \cdot \dfrac{\langle k^2 \rangle - \langle k \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}$ | SIR/SIS | Outbreak if $R_0 > 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall**: In table entries above, vertical bars $\vert$ are written as $\vert x \vert$ — never as the markdown pipe $\vert$ to avoid breaking table rendering. Use \vert or \mid.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Health Policy**: Identifying super-spreaders for vaccination in COVID-19, Ebola outbreaks.
- **Cybersecurity**: Modeling computer virus / malware propagation across the Internet and IoT networks.
- **Social Media Analytics**: Predicting viral cascades of misinformation (rumor spreading).
- **Power Grid Resilience**: Modeling cascading failures — analogous to SIS epidemics on grid topology.
- **Marketing & Influence Maximization**: Designing viral campaigns with minimum seeding cost.
- **Epidemic Forecasting**: Real-time $R_0$ estimation from contact-tracing networks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: SIS Heterogeneous Mean-Field Threshold $\tau_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 \rangle$

### Setup

Consider an uncorrelated random network with degree distribution $P(k)$. Let $\rho_k(t)$ be the **density of infected nodes among degree-class $k$** at time $t$. Each node has $k$ stubs connecting to the network. For an uncorrelated graph, the probability that a randomly chosen neighbor of a degree-$k$ node is itself of degree $k'$ equals $\frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle}$ (the **excess degree distribution**).

### Step 1 — Write the SIS Rate Equation

In the **heterogeneous mean-field approximation**, the time evolution of $\rho_k$ is:

$$
\frac{d\rho_k}{dt} = -\mu \rho_k + \lambda k (1 - \rho_k) \Theta
$$

**Textual logic**: The first term $-\mu \rho_k$ represents the **recovery outflow** — infected nodes in degree-class $k$ recover at rate $\mu$ per node. The second term $+\lambda k (1 - \rho_k) \Theta$ represents the **infection inflow** — a susceptible node in class $k$ has $k$ neighbors, each contact succeeds at rate $\lambda$, and the probability of being susceptible is $(1 - \rho_k)$, multiplied by $\Theta$, the probability that a randomly chosen neighbor is infected.

### Step 2 — Express $\Theta$ in Terms of $\rho_k$

The probability $\Theta$ that a randomly followed edge points to an infected node is:

$$
\Theta = \sum_{k'} \frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle} \rho_{k'}
$$

**Textual logic**: We weight each degree class by its excess degree $k'$ and normalize by the mean degree $\langle k \rangle$ because high-degree nodes are over-represented in the neighbor sample.

### Step 3 — Linearize Around the Disease-Free Equilibrium

At disease-free equilibrium, $\rho_k \approx 0$ for all $k$, so $(1 - \rho_k) \approx 1$. The equation becomes:

$$
\frac{d\rho_k}{dt} \approx -\mu \rho_k + \lambda k \Theta
$$

**Textual logic**: Near the threshold, infections are rare, so the **saturation** term $(1 - \rho_k)$ can be dropped. This linearization is valid only in the early-stage or near-threshold regime.

### Step 4 — Seek Exponential Solutions

Assume solutions of the form $\rho_k(t) = \epsilon_k e^{\alpha t}$ for some growth rate $\alpha$. Substituting:

$$
\alpha \epsilon_k = -\mu \epsilon_k + \lambda k \sum_{k'} \frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle} \epsilon_{k'}
$$

**Textual logic**: We hypothesize that the perturbation grows exponentially. The sum on the right is independent of $k$ — call it $C$.

### Step 5 — Solve for $\epsilon_k$

$$
\epsilon_k (\alpha + \mu) = \lambda k C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \epsilon_k = \frac{\lambda k C}{\alpha + \mu}
$$

**Textual logic**: Rearrange to express $\epsilon_k$ explicitly in terms of $k$. The constant $C$ cancels out below.

### Step 6 — Determine Self-Consistency via $\Theta$

Substitute $\epsilon_k$ back into the definition of $C$:

$$
C = \sum_{k'} \frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle} \epsilon_{k'} = \sum_{k'} \frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle} \cdot \frac{\lambda k' C}{\alpha + \mu}
$$

$$
C = \frac{\lambda C}{\alpha + \mu} \cdot \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle} \sum_{k'} k'^2 P(k')
$$

**Textual logic**: The $k' P(k')$ factor in the excess-degree weight combines with the $k'$ from $\epsilon_k$ to produce $k'^2$.

### Step 7 — Apply the Non-Trivial Solution Condition

For $C \neq 0$, divide both sides by $C$:

$$
1 = \frac{\lambda}{\alpha + \mu} \cdot \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}
$$

**Textual logic**: A non-zero growing perturbation requires this equation to hold. Rearrange:

$$
\alpha + \mu = \frac{\lambda \langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \alpha = \frac{\lambda \langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} - \mu
$$

### Step 8 — Apply the Outbreak Condition $\alpha > 0$

The outbreak occurs when $\alpha > 0$:

$$
\frac{\lambda \langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} > \mu \;\;\Leftrightarrow\;\; \lambda > \frac{\mu \langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle}
$$

### Step 9 — Final Threshold

$$
\tau_c = \frac{1}{\lambda_c} = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result**: The SIS epidemic threshold on an uncorrelated heterogeneous network is $\boxed{\tau_c = \langle k \rangle \,/\, \langle k^2 \rangle}$. For scale-free networks with $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$ and $2 < \gamma < 3$, $\langle k^2 \rangle \to \infty$, so $\tau_c \to 0$. **This means epidemics never die out naturally in large scale-free networks.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State Prevalence $\rho_\infty$ on Homogeneous Networks (SIS)

### Setup

For a **homogeneous** network ($\langle k^2 \rangle \approx \langle k \rangle^2$), the dynamics near equilibrium satisfy:

$$
\frac{d\rho}{dt} = -\mu \rho + \lambda \langle k \rangle \rho (1 - \rho) = 0
$$

### Step 1 — Solve for the Fixed Point

At steady state:

$$
\rho_\infty \left[ \lambda \langle k \rangle (1 - \rho_\infty) - \mu \right] = 0
$$

**Textual logic**: Either no infection ($\rho_\infty = 0$) or the bracket vanishes.

### Step 2 — Solve the Non-Trivial Branch

$$
\lambda \langle k \rangle (1 - \rho_\infty) = \mu \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 1 - \rho_\infty = \frac{\mu}{\lambda \langle k \rangle}
$$

$$
\rho_\infty = 1 - \frac{\mu}{\lambda \langle k \rangle} = 1 - \frac{\tau_c}{\lambda}
$$

**Textual logic**: Above threshold, the prevalence is the fractional distance above the critical transmissibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation**: This formula is valid only for $\lambda \gtrsim \tau_c$ (just above threshold). For $\lambda \gg \tau_c$, $\rho_\infty$ saturates near 1 and a more refined approach (quasi-chemical mean-field) is required.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: SIR Final Size via Self-Consistency

### Setup

In the SIR model, let $R_\infty$ be the **final attack rate** (fraction of population ever infected). The probability that a randomly chosen neighbor **escapes** infection is $u$, satisfying the self-consistency:

$$
u = 1 - T + T \cdot u^{\langle k \rangle}
$$

**Textual logic**: With probability $1-T$ the neighbor avoids infection, and with probability $T$ it is infected but each of its $\langle k \rangle$ other neighbors must also avoid being the infector. The mean-field treats neighbors as independent — a simplifying assumption.

### Step 1 — Express $R_\infty$ in Terms of $u$

A node escapes infection iff all its neighbors escape being infected:

$$
1 - R_\infty = u^{\langle k \rangle + 1} \approx u^{\langle k \rangle}
$$

**Textual logic**: We approximate $\langle k \rangle + 1 \approx \langle k \rangle$ for large $\langle k \rangle$.

### Step 2 — Eliminate $u$

From the self-consistency equation, $u \to u^{\langle k \rangle}$ relates to $(1-u)/T$. Solving explicitly:

$$
R_\infty = 1 - \exp\left(-\frac{\lambda}{\mu} R_\infty \langle k \rangle \right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **transcendental equation** in $R_\infty$. It must be solved numerically (or graphically). The trivial solution $R_\infty = 0$ always exists; a non-trivial solution appears only when $\lambda \langle k \rangle / \mu > 1$, confirming the threshold $\tau_c = \mu / \langle k \rangle$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

**Problem**: A scale-free network with $\gamma = 2.5$, $N = 10^4$, $k_{\min} = 1$, $k_{\max} = 100$, recovery rate $\mu = 0.1$, transmission $T = 0.05$.

Compute the threshold $\tau_c$:

$$
\tau_c = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle} \approx \frac{1}{\langle k^2 \rangle / \langle k \rangle}
$$

For $\gamma = 2.5$: $\langle k^2 \rangle / \langle k \rangle \approx k_{\max}^{3-\gamma} / (3-\gamma) \approx 100^{0.5} / 0.5 \approx 20$.

$$
\tau_c \approx \frac{1}{20} = 0.05
$$

Effective spreading rate:

$$
\lambda = \frac{T}{\mu} = \frac{0.05}{0.1} = 0.5
$$

Since $\lambda = 0.5 > \tau_c = 0.05$, **outbreak occurs** with margin $\lambda / \tau_c = 10$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the SIS and SIR epidemic models with a suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- **SIS Model (Susceptible-Infected-Susceptible)**: Nodes transition $S \to I \to S$, allowing **re-infection**. The infection persists endemically above threshold. *Example*: Common cold, computer viruses on the Internet.
- **SIR Model (Susceptible-Infected-Recovered)**: Nodes transition $S \to I \to R$ irreversibly; recovered nodes gain permanent immunity. *Example*: Measles, COVID-19 (post-immunity).
- **Key Difference**: SIS allows cyclic re-infection; SIR is a one-pass process leading to eventual disease extinction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the basic reproduction number $R_0$ and the epidemic threshold $\tau_c$. State their relationship.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:
- $R_0$: Expected number of secondary infections from a single infected node in a fully susceptible network.
- $\tau_c$: Minimum transmissibility required for an outbreak to spread macroscopically.
- **Relationship**: An outbreak occurs iff $R_0 > 1$ iff $\lambda > \tau_c$, where $\lambda = T/\mu$.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern**: Each Part B question has internal choice. Both options below are designed as full 14-mark questions with two 7-mark sub-parts each.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the epidemic threshold $\tau_c$ for the **SIS model** on a heterogeneous uncorrelated network using the heterogeneous mean-field approximation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the result from (a), explain why scale-free networks with $2 < \gamma < 3$ exhibit **vanishing epidemic thresholds**. What are the public-health implications? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a) — Derivation of $\tau_c$ (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark)** — **Setup**: On uncorrelated network with degree distribution $P(k)$, let $\rho_k(t)$ be the infected fraction in degree-class $k$.

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — **Write rate equation**:

$$
\frac{d\rho_k}{dt} = -\mu \rho_k + \lambda k (1 - \rho_k) \Theta
$$

where $\Theta = \sum_{k'} \frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle} \rho_{k'}$ is the infected-neighbor probability.

**Step 3 (1 mark)** — **Linearize**: Near disease-free state, $(1-\rho_k) \approx 1$.

$$
\frac{d\rho_k}{dt} \approx -\mu \rho_k + \lambda k \Theta
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — **Exponential ansatz**: Let $\rho_k(t) = \epsilon_k e^{\alpha t}$.

$$
\epsilon_k = \frac{\lambda k C}{\alpha + \mu}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark)** — **Self-consistency**:

$$
1 = \frac{\lambda \langle k^2 \rangle}{(\alpha + \mu) \langle k \rangle}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark)** — **Apply $\alpha = 0$ threshold condition**:

$$
\boxed{\tau_c = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle}}
$$

---

#### Model Solution to A(b) — Scale-Free Threshold Collapse (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — **Compute moments for $P(k) \sim k^{-\gamma}$**:

$$
\langle k \rangle = \int_{k_{\min}}^{k_{\max}} k^{1-\gamma} dk, \quad \langle k^2 \rangle = \int_{k_{\min}}^{k_{\max}} k^{2-\gamma} dk
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — **Convergence behavior**: For $2 < \gamma < 3$, the integral $\int k^{2-\gamma} dk$ **diverges** at the upper limit as $k_{\max} \to \infty$. Therefore $\langle k^2 \rangle \to \infty$ as $N \to \infty$.

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — **Threshold collapse**:

$$
\tau_c = \frac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle} \to 0 \quad \text{as } N \to \infty
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — **Public-health implications**:
- Standard vaccination thresholds ($R_0 < 1$) derived from homogeneous models are **inadequate**.
- Hubs act as **super-spreaders** and must be prioritized.
- Targeted immunization is dramatically more effective than random.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare **random immunization** and **targeted (hub-based) immunization** strategies on a scale-free network. Derive expressions for the critical immunized fraction $g_c$ in both cases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider an SIS epidemic on an Erdős–Rényi graph with $N = 1000$, $\langle k \rangle = 6$, $\mu = 0.2$, $T = 0.05$. Compute (i) the effective spreading rate $\lambda$, (ii) the epidemic threshold $\tau_c$, (iii) the steady-state prevalence $\rho_\infty$. Is the system super-critical? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a) — Immunization Strategies (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — **Random Immunization**: A fraction $g$ of nodes is randomly removed. The effective transmissibility becomes $\lambda' = \lambda(1-g)$. The outbreak is suppressed when $\lambda' < \tau_c$:

$$
g_c^{(\text{random})} = 1 - \frac{\tau_c}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — **Targeted Immunization**: Immunize all nodes with degree $k > k_c$. The network's effective $\langle k^2 \rangle$ is dramatically reduced. The threshold becomes:

$$
\tau_c^{(\text{targeted})} = \frac{\langle k \rangle_{g}}{\langle k^2 \rangle_{g}}
$$

where the moments are computed over the residual (unimmunized) network.

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — **Comparison on Scale-Free**:
- Random $g_c$ for scale-free networks can exceed **80%** — practically infeasible.
- Targeted $g_c$ is typically **< 20%** because removing hubs disconnects the network.
- Even $\sim 5\%$ hub removal can push $\tau_c \to 1$ on highly heterogeneous networks.

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — **Conclusion**: Targeted immunization exploits network topology for cost-effective disease control.

---

#### Model Solution to B(b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Given**: $N = 1000$, $\langle k \rangle = 6$, $\mu = 0.2$, $T = 0.05$.

**Step 1 (2 marks)** — **Effective spreading rate**:

$$
\lambda = \frac{T}{\mu} = \frac{0.05}{0.2} = 0.25
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks)** — **Epidemic threshold (HMF for homogeneous)**:

$$
\tau_c = \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle} = \frac{1}{6} \approx 0.1667
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks)** — **Steady-state prevalence**:

$$
\rho_\infty = 1 - \frac{\tau_c}{\lambda} = 1 - \frac{0.1667}{0.25} = 1 - 0.6667 = 0.3333
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark)** — **Conclusion**: Since $\lambda = 0.25 > \tau_c = 0.1667$, the system is **super-critical**. Approximately **33.3%** of the population remains infected in the endemic state.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Threshold formula mix-up**: Students confuse $\tau_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 \rangle$ (HET) with $\tau_c = 1/\langle k \rangle$ (HMF). Read the question carefully — if the network is scale-free, use HET.
> 2. **Forgot $\mu$ in spreading rate**: $\lambda = T/\mu$, not just $T$. Many students lose 1 mark for missing the recovery rate.
> 3. **Sign error in linearization**: When substituting $(1-\rho_k) \approx 1$, students sometimes incorrectly drop the $-\mu \rho_k$ term. Both terms must remain.
> 4. **Misinterpreting prevalence**: $\rho_\infty$ is the *fraction* infected, not the *number*. Don't forget the $1/N$ conversion if asked for absolute count.
> 5. **Scale-free moment divergence**: Many students assume $\langle k^2 \rangle$ is finite for all $\gamma$. It diverges only for $\gamma \le 3$.
> 6. **Forgetting to compare $\lambda$ vs $\tau_c$**: A numerical problem may give a $\lambda$ below threshold — students must explicitly state "sub-critical, no outbreak."

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three canonical models**: SI (irreversible), SIS (cyclic), SIR (immune). Know state diagrams and one example each.
- ✅ **Two fundamental parameters**: Transmissibility $T$ and recovery rate $\mu$. Effective spreading rate $\lambda = T/\mu$.
- ✅ **Threshold for SIS on HMF (homogeneous)**: $\tau_c = 1/\langle k \rangle$.
- ✅ **Threshold for SIS on HET (heterogeneous)**: $\tau_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 \rangle$.
- ✅ **Scale-free collapse**: For $2 < \gamma \le 3$, $\tau_c \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. No natural herd immunity.
- ✅ **SIR final size equation**: $R_\infty = 1 - \exp(-\lambda R_\infty \langle k \rangle / \mu)$ — transcendental, solve numerically or graphically.
- ✅ **Heterogeneous mean-field (HMF)** approximates $\rho_k(t)$ per degree class.
- ✅ **Excess degree distribution**: $\frac{k' P(k')}{\langle k \rangle}$ — used in deriving $\Theta$.
- ✅ **Outbreak condition**: $\lambda > \tau_c$ equivalently $R_0 > 1$.
- ✅ **Steady-state SIS prevalence** (HMF): $\rho_\infty = 1 - \tau_c/\lambda$ valid near threshold.
- ✅ **Random immunization**: $g_c = 1 - \tau_c / \lambda$; expensive on scale-free.
- ✅ **Targeted immunization**: Immunize hubs first; much lower $g_c$.
- ✅ **Acquaintance immunization**: Immunize random neighbors of random nodes — surprisingly efficient.
- ✅ **Bond percolation equivalence**: SIR = bond percolation with $T$ as occupation probability; final size = giant component.
- ✅ **Spectral interpretation**: $\tau_c = 1 / \Lambda_{\max}$ for general networks.
- ✅ **Real-world examples**: COVID-19 (SIR with waning immunity → SIS-like), rumor cascades, malware spread, power-grid cascades.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip**: Always specify which mean-field approximation (HMF/HET/quasi-chemical) you are using — examiners award marks for clarity of assumption.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Susceptible-Infected (SI) Model on a Network
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT754 - NETWORK SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Susceptible-Infected (SI) Model on a Network

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Susceptible-Infected (SI) Model on a Network** is a fundamental compartmental epidemiological model from network science that describes the spread of an infectious agent (or information, rumor, computer virus, or contagion of any kind) across a population structured as a graph. In this model:

- **Nodes** represent individual entities (people, computers, web pages, etc.).
- **Edges** represent the contact/interaction channels through which transmission can occur.
- Each node exists in one of exactly two discrete states: **Susceptible (S)** — healthy and capable of contracting the contagion, or **Infected (I)** — already carrying and capable of transmitting the contagion.
- A susceptible node connected to one or more infected neighbors transitions to the infected state with a per-contact transmission rate **β** (often called the *infection rate* or *spreading rate*).
- Critically, the **SI model assumes NO recovery** — once a node is infected, it remains infected forever. The total infected population is therefore a non-decreasing, monotonically increasing function of time, bounded above by **N** (the total network size).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The SI model is the *parent* model of all compartmental models (SIS, SIR, SEIR). In KTU Module 4, it is the gateway topic to studying how **network topology** (degree distribution, clustering, degree correlations) fundamentally alters the dynamics of spreading processes compared to the classical well-mixed (mean-field) assumption.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **gossip chain in a college classroom**. You are a "Susceptible" person — you haven't heard the rumor yet. Once a friend sitting next to you whispers the gossip, you become "Infected" (you now know it, and more importantly, you are *willing to spread it further*). Crucially, **you never forget the gossip** — there is no recovery. 

In a *well-mixed* classroom (everyone talks to everyone equally), the gossip spreads like an exponential fire. But in a real classroom (some students are popular with 20 friends, others are quiet with 2 friends), the **structure of friendships** matters enormously. The popular students are super-spreaders of gossip. The SI model on a network formalizes this: the rate of gossip spread depends not just on *how infectious* the rumor is (**β**), but also on *who knows whom* — encoded in the adjacency matrix **A** of the contact network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SI and not just SIR?** In many real systems — computer worm propagation on the early internet, the spread of a permanent piece of misinformation, or viral marketing campaigns — the contagion is **irreversible**. The SI model captures these dynamics exactly, and serves as the theoretical bedrock for more elaborate models.

### 1.3 Key Parameters and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics govern the SI model dynamics:

- **N** — Total number of nodes in the network.
- **β** — Per-edge transmission rate (dimension: 1/time).
- **⟨k⟩** — Average degree of the network.
- **⟨k²⟩** — Second moment of the degree distribution.
- **ρ(t)** or **I(t)/N** — Fraction of infected nodes at time *t* (epidemic prevalence).
- **T = 1 − e^(−βt)** — Bond transmissibility over contact time *t*.
- **λ = β/μ** — Basic spreading rate (when recovery rate μ exists); for SI, μ = 0.
- **τ_c** — Epidemic threshold (critical transmission rate).
- **R₀** — Basic reproduction number (average number of secondary infections per infected node in a fully susceptible population).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Theoretical Frameworks for SI on Networks

The dynamics of the SI model on a network can be analyzed at three increasing levels of mathematical precision:

#### Framework 1: Homogeneous Mean-Field (HMF) Approach
This is the simplest approximation. It assumes that the network is **sufficiently random and homogeneous** that every node can be treated as having the same effective environment. We track only the global fraction of infected nodes, *i(t)* = I(t)/N.

- It ignores degree heterogeneity.
- It is the network analogue of the classical mass-action Kermack-McKendrick equation.
- **Validity:** Only accurate for Erdős–Rényi random graphs in the limit of large N, or for very homogeneous networks.

#### Framework 2: Heterogeneous Mean-Field (HMF) Approach
This is the workhorse of network epidemiology. It classifies nodes by their **degree *k*** and tracks the infected fraction *I_k(t)* within each degree class.

- It captures the crucial role of **hubs** (high-degree nodes) as super-spreaders.
- It uses the degree-degree correlation assumption (typically annealed, meaning edges are randomly rewired).
- **Validity:** Good for random configuration model networks with low clustering and no degree correlations.

#### Framework 3: Quenched Mean-Field (QMF) Approach
The most accurate (and computationally expensive) approach. It tracks the *actual* infection state of every individual node *i* in the network, respecting the precise adjacency structure.

- It uses the actual adjacency matrix **A** of the network.
- It is essentially a deterministic ODE system of size N.
- **Validity:** Exact in the limit of large N with the annealed approximation replaced by the actual quenched network structure.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why degree-class equations?** Because in a heterogeneous network, a node of degree 10 has 10 chances of being infected per unit time, while a node of degree 2 has only 2. Pooling them together would erase this vital information.
- **Why the annealed approximation?** It allows us to replace the actual discrete dynamics of which exact neighbor is infected with the *probability* that a randomly chosen neighbor is infected, namely **Θ(t) = (1/⟨k⟩) Σ_k k P(k) I_k(t)**. This is the probability that following a random edge leads to an infected node.
- **Why no epidemic threshold in the pure SI model?** Because without recovery, the disease *always* eventually infects everyone (i.e., *i(t → ∞) → 1*) on a connected network. However, the *time scale* of approach to full infection is governed by an effective threshold related to the leading eigenvalue of the adjacency matrix.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Equation** | **Meaning / Statement** | **Domain of Validity** |
|---|---|---|
| $i(t) = I(t)/N$ | Fraction of infected nodes at time $t$ | All frameworks |
| $\dfrac{di}{dt} = \beta \langle k \rangle \, i(1-i)$ | HMF ODE (homogeneous networks) | ER random graphs, $N \to \infty$ |
| $i(t) = \dfrac{i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}{1 - i_0 + i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}$ | Closed-form HMF solution | HMF regime |
| $\dfrac{dI_k}{dt} = \beta k \, (1 - I_k) \, \Theta(t)$ | Degree-class HMF ODE | Configuration model, annealed |
| $\Theta(t) = \dfrac{1}{\langle k \rangle} \sum_{k} k \, P(k) \, I_k(t)$ | Probability a random edge leads to an infected node | Annealed approximation |
| $\dfrac{d\Theta}{dt} = \beta \, \dfrac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} \, \Theta (1 - \Theta)$ | Effective HMF for $\Theta$ (closed chain) | Configuration model |
| $\lambda_c = \tau_c = \dfrac{\langle k \rangle}{\langle k^2 \rangle}$ | Epidemic threshold (inverse of leading eigenvalue) | SIS/SIR limit; for SI gives *time-scale* crossover |
| $\dfrac{di_i}{dt} = \beta \sum_{j} A_{ij} \, x_j \, (1 - x_i)$ | QMF equation for node $i$ | Exact (deterministic) |
| $T = 1 - e^{-\beta t}$ | Bond transmissibility over time $t$ | Maps SI to bond percolation |
| $S_{\infty} = G_1(1-T)$ | Final size of outbreak (fraction susceptible at $t \to \infty$) | Configuration model, percolation equivalence |
| $G_0(x) = \sum_k P(k) x^k$ | PGF of degree distribution | Network generation |
| $G_1(x) = G_0^{\prime}(x)/G_0^{\prime}(1)$ | Excess degree PGF | Network analysis |
| $R_0 = \beta \langle k \rangle / \mu$ | Basic reproduction number (limit $\mu \to 0$ gives $\infty$ for SI) | General compartmental models |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Note:** In the HMF equation, the symbol $\vert$ inside tables has been replaced with $\mid$ or removed to preserve the markdown table structure. Students writing in LaTeX should use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cybersecurity:** Modeling worm propagation in computer networks (the *Code Red* and *Slammer* worms essentially followed SI dynamics on the router-graph).
- **Public Health:** Early-stage outbreak analysis (before recovery is observed), e.g., the initial 2014-Ebola outbreak.
- **Marketing & Sociology:** Modeling viral content spread on Twitter/X, YouTube, or TikTok (an infected user = a user who has shared the content; they never "unshare").
- **Infrastructure Networks:** Modeling cascading failures in power grids or transportation networks where a failed node stays failed.
- **Neuroscience:** Modeling signal propagation in neural networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Homogeneous Mean-Field (HMF) SI Model

**Step 1 — Setup.** Let *N* be the total number of nodes, *I(t)* be the number of infected nodes at time *t*, and *S(t) = N − I(t)* be the number of susceptible nodes. The fraction of infected nodes is *i(t) = I(t)/N*.

**Step 2 — Rate of new infections.** In a homogeneous network, each node has an average degree *⟨k⟩*. An infected node transmits to each of its neighbors with rate *β*. The probability that a randomly contacted neighbor is susceptible is *S(t)/N = 1 − i(t)*. The total rate of new infections is therefore:

$$
\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \langle k \rangle \cdot \frac{I}{N} \cdot \frac{S}{N} \cdot N
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to fractions.** Dividing both sides by *N* and substituting *S/N = 1 − i*:

$$
\frac{di}{dt} = \beta \langle k \rangle \, i(1-i)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the logistic equation.** This is the classic logistic differential equation with intrinsic growth rate *r = β⟨k⟩* and carrying capacity *K = 1*.

**Step 5 — Solve by separation of variables.** Write:

$$
\frac{di}{i(1-i)} = \beta \langle k \rangle \, dt
$$

Using partial fractions: $1/[i(1-i)] = 1/i + 1/(1-i)$. Integrating both sides:

$$
\int \left( \frac{1}{i} + \frac{1}{1-i} \right) di = \int \beta \langle k \rangle \, dt
$$

$$
\ln \left| \frac{i}{1-i} \right| = \beta \langle k \rangle \, t + C
$$

**Step 6 — Apply initial condition.** At *t = 0*, *i(0) = i₀*. This gives *C = ln[i₀/(1−i₀)]*. Therefore:

$$
\ln \left( \frac{i}{1-i} \cdot \frac{1-i_0}{i_0} \right) = \beta \langle k \rangle \, t
$$

**Step 7 — Exponentiate and solve for i(t).**

$$
\frac{i}{1-i} = \frac{i_0}{1-i_0} \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}
$$

$$
i(t) = \frac{i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}{1 - i_0 + i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}
$$

**Step 8 — Verify limiting behavior.** As $t \to \infty$, the exponential term dominates, so $i(t) \to 1$. ✓ At $t=0$, $i(0) = i_0$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Heterogeneous Mean-Field (HMF) — Degree-Class Equations

**Step 1 — Classify nodes by degree.** Let $I_k(t)$ be the density (fraction) of infected nodes among nodes of degree $k$ at time $t$. The number of nodes of degree $k$ is $N \cdot P(k)$.

**Step 2 — Probability of infection for a degree-k node.** A degree-$k$ node has $k$ neighbors. Each neighbor is infected with probability $\Theta(t)$. The probability that *none* of the $k$ neighbors is infected is $(1-\Theta)^k$. The probability of becoming infected in time $dt$ is $\beta$ times the number of infected neighbors. In the annealed (probabilistic) approximation, the rate of infection is:

$$
\frac{dI_k}{dt} = \beta k (1 - I_k) \Theta(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Define Θ(t).** A random edge in the configuration model leads to a node whose degree is drawn from the *excess degree distribution* $Q(k) = kP(k)/\langle k \rangle$. The probability that such a node is infected is $\sum_k Q(k) I_k(t)$:

$$
\Theta(t) = \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle} \sum_k k P(k) I_k(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Closed system of equations.** Equations (Step 2) for all $k$ and Equation (Step 3) form a closed set of $k_{\max}+1$ coupled ODEs.

**Step 5 — Early-time linearization.** For early times, $I_k \ll 1$, so we drop the $(1-I_k)$ term:

$$
\frac{dI_k}{dt} \approx \beta k \Theta(t)
$$

Since $\Theta$ is itself linear in $I_k$ at early times, we obtain the linearized system whose solution is dominated by the largest eigenvalue. The associated growth rate is:

$$
\Lambda_{\max} = \beta \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} - \langle k \rangle
$$

For the SI model, the dominant growth rate of $\Theta$ at early times is:

$$
\Lambda_{\text{SI}} = \beta \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle}
$$

**Step 6 — Effective single ODE for Θ.** Differentiate the expression for $\Theta$ and use the degree-class equation:

$$
\frac{d\Theta}{dt} = \frac{1}{\langle k \rangle} \sum_k k^2 P(k) \cdot \beta (1 - I_k) \Theta
$$

In the early-time regime (and at late times when infection is widespread), one can derive an effective closed ODE. The result is:

$$
\frac{d\Theta}{dt} = \beta \frac{\langle k^2 \rangle}{\langle k \rangle} \Theta(1 - \Theta)
$$

This is again logistic, with effective growth rate $\beta \langle k^2 \rangle / \langle k \rangle$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Quenched Mean-Field (QMF) — Per-Node Equation

**Step 1 — Define per-node state variable.** Let $x_i(t) \in [0,1]$ be the probability that node $i$ is infected at time $t$. The discrete stochastic dynamics is replaced by a continuous deterministic one (valid for $N \to \infty$).

**Step 2 — Construct the ODE.** Node $i$ is infected if it is susceptible $(1-x_i)$ AND at least one of its neighbors $j$ is infected and transmits. The instantaneous rate of infection of node $i$ is:

$$
\frac{dx_i}{dt} = \beta (1 - x_i) \sum_{j=1}^{N} A_{ij} x_j
$$

The sum $\sum_j A_{ij} x_j$ counts the *expected number of infected neighbors* of node $i$.

**Step 3 — Vector-matrix form.** Let $\mathbf{x}(t) = (x_1, \dots, x_N)^T$. The system is:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} = \beta \left( \mathbf{I} - \text{diag}(\mathbf{x}) \right) \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 4 — Early-time linearization.** For $x_i \ll 1$:

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{x}}{dt} \approx \beta \mathbf{A} \mathbf{x}
$$

**Step 5 — Spectral solution.** The solution is dominated by the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_1$ of the adjacency matrix $\mathbf{A}$:

$$
\mathbf{x}(t) \sim c_1 \, \mathbf{v}_1 \, e^{\beta \lambda_1 t} \quad \text{as } t \to \infty
$$

where $\mathbf{v}_1$ is the leading eigenvector (Perron-Frobenius eigenvector). This is the **most important result** of QMF: the time scale of the SI spreading is set by $\lambda_1$, the **spectral radius** of the network's adjacency matrix.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Mapping to Bond Percolation

**Step 1 — Probability of transmission along an edge.** In the SI model with constant rate $\beta$, the probability that a particular edge transmits the contagion during a time interval of length $t$ is $T = 1 - e^{-\beta t}$ (assuming the source node was infected at time 0).

**Step 2 — Independence assumption.** The transmission along each edge is independent of the others in the annealed picture.

**Step 3 — Mapping.** The final state of the SI model (as $t \to \infty$) on a network is exactly equivalent to **bond percolation** on the same network with bond occupation probability $T$.

**Step 4 — Final size formula.** In the configuration model, the probability that a randomly chosen node is *not* in the giant component reached from the initial seed is $u = G_1(1-Tu)$. Solving for $u$ gives the *final size* (fraction of network not infected). The final infected fraction is:

$$
R_\infty = 1 - G_0(u)
$$

where $G_0$ and $G_1$ are the PGFs of the degree and excess degree distributions, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bond Percolation Equivalence** is a board-favorite question. The two key takeaways are: (a) the mapping is exact in the annealed limit; (b) the final size depends on whether $T$ exceeds the percolation threshold $T_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 \rangle$.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Framework** | **State Variable** | **ODE** | **Growth Rate** | **Threshold** | **Computational Cost** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| HMF | $i(t)$ | $\dot{i} = \beta \langle k \rangle i(1-i)$ | $\beta \langle k \rangle$ | None (full infection eventually) | $O(1)$ |
| Degree-class HMF | $I_k(t), \Theta(t)$ | $\dot{I}_k = \beta k (1-I_k) \Theta$ | $\beta \langle k^2 \rangle / \langle k \rangle$ | $\lambda_c = \langle k \rangle / \langle k^2 \rangle$ | $O(k_{\max})$ |
| QMF | $x_i(t)$ | $\dot{x}_i = \beta(1-x_i)\sum_j A_{ij} x_j$ | $\beta \lambda_1(\mathbf{A})$ | $1/\lambda_1$ | $O(N^2)$ or $O(Nk)$ sparse |
| Bond Percolation | $u, T$ | $u = G_1(1-Tu)$ | Final size formula | $T_c = \langle k \rangle/\langle k^2 \rangle$ | $O(1)$ after PGFs |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 State Transition Diagram — The SI Compartmental Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the compartmental state-transition diagram of the SI model. Each state is a node, and the directed edge represents the transition governed by the force of infection.

```mermaid
graph LR
    susA["S : SUSCEPTIBLE"] -- "beta per contact" --> infA["I : INFECTED"]
    infA2["I : INFECTED"] -. "absorbing: no recovery" .- susA
    classDef sState fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iState fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class susA sState
    class infA,infA2 iState
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A node in state **S** (blue) transitions irreversibly to state **I** (red) at per-contact rate **β** upon contact with an infected neighbor. Once in state **I**, the node stays there forever — it is an **absorbing state** for the individual node (dashed self-loop convention).

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SI on a Network

This diagram shows the *information-processing* architecture of how the SI model evaluates transitions on a network, from a single node's perspective.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inputA["Input: Node i state and adjacency list"] --> checkA{"Is node i Susceptible?"}
    checkA -- "NO" --> noOpA["No transition: stay Infected"]
    checkA -- "YES" --> countA["Count infected neighbors: sum A_ij * x_j"]
    countA --> rateA["Compute transition rate: beta times infected-neighbor-count"]
    rateA --> integrateA["Integrate ODE: dx_i per unit time"]
    integrateA --> outputA["Output: Updated x_i at time t plus dt"]
    outputA --> loopA["Loop over all nodes i in 1 to N"]
    loopA --> checkA
    noOpA --> loopA
    classDef decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef terminal fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class checkA decision
    class countA,rateA,integrateA,outputA,loopA process
    class inputA,noOpA terminal
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is the QMF (Quenched Mean-Field) algorithmic flow. For *every* node $i$ in the network at *every* time step, the algorithm checks the state, computes the rate of new infection using the *actual* adjacency structure, and updates the state. The loop continues until the global infection fraction stabilizes (or reaches a specified final time).

---

### 4.3 Degree-Class Mean-Field Coupling Diagram

This shows how the degree-class equations interlink through the global quantity $\Theta(t)$.

```mermaid
graph TD
    theta["Theta of t: probability edge leads to infected node"] --> i1["I_1 of t: infected fraction of degree 1 nodes"]
    theta --> i2["I_2 of t: infected fraction of degree 2 nodes"]
    theta --> i3["I_3 of t: infected fraction of degree 3 nodes"]
    theta --> iK["I_k of t: infected fraction of degree k nodes"]
    i1 --> theta
    i2 --> theta
    i3 --> theta
    iK --> theta
    classDef global fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef degreeClass fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class theta global
    class i1,i2,i3,iK degreeClass
```

> **Reading the diagram:** $\Theta(t)$ (purple, global) drives the infection rate of *every* degree class $I_k$ (blue, local), and every degree class feeds back into $\Theta$ through the excess-degree sum. The mutual arrows indicate the **closed system of coupled ODEs** that defines the HMF framework.

---

### 4.4 SI vs. SIS vs. SIR Comparison Flowchart

This contextual diagram situates SI within the broader family of compartmental models.

```mermaid
graph LR
    siA["SI: S to I only, no recovery"] 
    sisA["SIS: S to I, I to S recovery"]
    sirA["SIR: S to I, I to R, no return to S"]
    siA --> sisA
    siA --> sirA
    sisA --> sirA
    classDef root fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class siA,sisA,sirA root
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

Define the **Susceptible-Infected (SI) Model** on a network. State clearly the meaning of the two states and the per-contact transmission rate **β**. Why is the SI model considered to have **no epidemic threshold** in a connected network?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Definition:** The SI model is a compartmental epidemiological model on a graph where each node exists in one of two states — **Susceptible (S)** (has not yet been infected) or **Infected (I)** (already carries the contagion and can transmit it).
- **(1 mark) Transmission:** A susceptible node connected to an infected neighbor becomes infected with per-contact rate **β** (dimension: inverse time). Once infected, the node **never recovers** — the **I** state is absorbing.
- **(1 mark) Threshold absence:** Because there is no recovery, on any connected network, the contagion eventually reaches every node. Hence $i(t \to \infty) \to 1$ regardless of how small **β** is (as long as **β > 0** and the network is connected). The notion of an epidemic threshold (as in SIS or SIR) does not apply; only a *time-scale* crossover exists.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

Distinguish between the **Homogeneous Mean-Field (HMF)** and **Heterogeneous Mean-Field (degree-class)** approaches to the SI model on a network. In which type of network is each approach most accurate?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark) HMF:** Tracks only the global infected fraction $i(t) = I(t)/N$ assuming every node behaves identically with effective contact rate $\beta \langle k \rangle$. The ODE is $di/dt = \beta \langle k \rangle \, i(1-i)$.
- **(1 mark) Degree-class HMF:** Tracks the infected fraction $I_k(t)$ separately for each degree class $k$, coupled through the global edge-probability $\Theta(t)$. The system is $\dot{I}_k = \beta k (1 - I_k) \Theta$.
- **(1 mark) Validity:** HMF is most accurate on **Erdős–Rényi (ER) random graphs** or other homogeneous networks. Degree-class HMF is most accurate on **random configuration model networks** with heterogeneous degree distributions (e.g., scale-free networks), where it captures the role of hubs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — HMF Derivation + Closed-Form Solution

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the **Homogeneous Mean-Field (HMF) differential equation** for the SI model on a homogeneous network of *N* nodes with average degree *⟨k⟩* and per-contact transmission rate *β*. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the ODE obtained in (a) using the initial condition $i(0) = i_0$ and obtain a closed-form expression for $i(t)$. Discuss the **limiting behavior** as $t \to \infty$ and as $t \to 0$. Compute the *epidemic half-time* $t_{1/2}$ (the time at which $i(t) = 0.5$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Setup and assumptions:** 
  - *Assumption 1:* The network is sufficiently random and homogeneous that all nodes are statistically equivalent.
  - *Assumption 2:* The fraction of infected nodes among the neighbors of any node equals the global fraction $i(t) = I(t)/N$ (annealed approximation).
  - *Assumption 3:* The system is large enough ($N \to \infty$) that stochastic fluctuations can be ignored; $i(t)$ is a deterministic continuous variable.
  
- **(2 marks) Rate of new infections:** 
  - The total number of *edges* in the network is $N \langle k \rangle / 2$. 
  - The probability that a random edge connects an *infected* node to a *susceptible* node is approximately $i(t) \cdot (1 - i(t))$. 
  - Per edge, the transmission rate is $\beta$. So the total new-infection rate is:
  
  $$\frac{dI}{dt} = \beta \cdot \frac{N \langle k \rangle}{2} \cdot i(t) \cdot (1 - i(t)) \cdot \frac{2}{N}$$
  
  The factor $2/N$ converts the edge-count to per-node rate. Simplifying:

  $$\frac{di}{dt} = \beta \langle k \rangle \, i(t) \left[ 1 - i(t) \right]$$

- **(1 mark) Identification:** Recognize as the **logistic equation** with intrinsic rate $r = \beta \langle k \rangle$ and carrying capacity $K = 1$.

- **(2 marks) Interpretation:** 
  - Early ($i \ll 1$): exponential growth $\sim e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}$.
  - Late ($i \to 1$): saturation due to depletion of susceptibles.

**(b) Solution and analysis (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Separation of variables:**
  
  $$\int_{i_0}^{i(t)} \frac{di'}{i'(1-i')} = \int_0^t \beta \langle k \rangle \, dt'$$

- **(1 mark) Partial fractions:** $1/[i(1-i)] = 1/i + 1/(1-i)$.

- **(1 mark) Integration:**

  $$\left[ \ln i' - \ln(1-i') \right]_{i_0}^{i(t)} = \beta \langle k \rangle t$$

  $$\ln \left( \frac{i(t)}{1-i(t)} \cdot \frac{1-i_0}{i_0} \right) = \beta \langle k \rangle t$$

- **(1 mark) Closed-form solution:**

  $$i(t) = \frac{i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}{1 - i_0 + i_0 \, e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t}}$$

- **(1 mark) Limiting behavior:**
  - As $t \to \infty$: $e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t} \to \infty$, so $i(t) \to 1$. The entire network becomes infected.
  - As $t \to 0$: $e^{\beta \langle k \rangle t} \to 1$, so $i(t) \to i_0$. The initial condition is recovered.

- **(1 mark) Half-time calculation:** Set $i(t_{1/2}) = 0.5$:

  $$0.5 = \frac{i_0 e^{r t_{1/2}}}{1 - i_0 + i_0 e^{r t_{1/2}}}$$
  
  Cross-multiplying and simplifying:
  
  $$0.5 (1 - i_0) = 0.5 \, i_0 \, e^{r t_{1/2}} \implies 1 - i_0 = i_0 \, e^{r t_{1/2}}$$
  
  $$e^{r t_{1/2}} = \frac{1 - i_0}{i_0}$$
